<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Swarm Intelligence — Formal Academic Definition

**Swarm Intelligence (SI)** is the collective behaviour of decentralised, self-organised systems, natural or artificial, in which simple agents interacting locally with one another and with their environment produce emergent global patterns. The concept was formally introduced by **Bonabeau, Dorigo, and Theraulaz in 1999** in their seminal work *"Swarm Intelligence: From Natural to Artificial Systems"*.

$$SI = \{ A, \Phi, \Omega, \Psi \}$$

Where:
- $A$ = Set of autonomous agents (e.g., ants, bees, birds, fish)
- $\Phi$ = Local interaction rules
- $\Omega$ = Environment (with modification capability — stigmergy)
- $\Psi$ = Global emergent objective function

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Swarm Intelligence is categorised under *Bio-Inspired Computing Paradigms*. It sits as a sub-discipline of **Computational Intelligence (CI)** and **Artificial Intelligence (AI)**, distinguishing itself by drawing principles from **ethology** (study of animal behaviour) rather than from symbolic logic or statistical learning alone.

## 1.2 Biological Foundations of Swarm Intelligence

A *swarm* in nature refers to a **large, loosely organised group of similar individuals** that coordinate through decentralised control. Notable biological examples include:

| Biological System | Real-World Example | Optimization Analogue |
|-------------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| Ant Colonies | Foraging, brood sorting | Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) |
| Bee Colonies | Waggle dance, nest site selection | Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) |
| Bird Flocks | V-formation, predator evasion | Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) |
| Fish Schools | Schooling behaviour | Fish School Search (FSS) |
| Bacterial Colonies | Chemotaxis | Bacterial Foraging Optimization (BFO) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Blind Ant Highway"**
> Imagine **1000 blindfolded people** scattered in a dark field, each holding a faint perfume bottle. They are searching for the **nearest restaurant** (food source). When one of them stumbles upon food, they spray perfume on the path back. Other blindfolded people, sensing stronger perfume on one trail, statistically follow it. Over time, the **shortest path accumulates the most perfume** (because ants travel faster over short paths and reinforce them more often). No single person knows the global map; the shortest route **emerges** purely from local scent-following.

This is the essence of swarm intelligence: **global optimum emerges from local, simple rules without central control**.

## 1.4 Core Properties of Swarm-Intelligent Systems (KTU Board-Favourite)

The four pillars of any swarm-intelligent system as defined in KTU textbooks are:

1. **Self-Organisation** — Global order arises from local interactions, not top-down commands.
2. **Stigmergy** — Indirect communication mediated by environmental modification (e.g., pheromone trails).
3. **Division of Labour** — Specialised roles emerge (e.g., foragers, soldiers, nurses in ant colonies).
4. **Decentralised Control** — No single agent is a leader; the swarm is **fault-tolerant**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These properties make SI algorithms ideal for **NP-hard combinatorial optimization** (TSP, VRP, scheduling), **dynamic routing in telecommunications**, and **swarm robotics**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanisms of Self-Organisation (Bonabeau et al. Framework)

Self-organisation in biological swarms relies on **four interacting mechanisms**. These are **guaranteed high-yield** in KTU examinations:

### Mechanism 1: Positive Feedback (Reinforcement)
- Promotes the creation of structures (e.g., pheromone trails).
- Simple rule: "If you find food, lay more pheromone."
- Risk: **Premature convergence** to a suboptimal path.

### Mechanism 2: Negative Feedback (Counter-Balance)
- Dissolves structures (e.g., pheromone **evaporation**).
- Prevents stagnation; promotes exploration.
- Implemented as the **evaporation rate** $\rho$ in ACO.

### Mechanism 3: Randomness (Stochastic Fluctuations)
- Introduces exploration; allows discovery of new paths.
- Prevents the system from locking into one solution.
- Modelled via **random proportional rule** in artificial ants.

### Mechanism 4: Multiple Interactions (Amplification)
- Microscopic local rules amplified into macroscopic global patterns.
- Number of agents $\to$ thousands, amplifying weak signals.

## 2.2 Stigmergy — The Pillar of Indirect Communication

**Stigmergy** was first defined by **Pierre-Paul Grassé (1959)** while studying termite nest construction. It is a class of mechanisms mediated by **environmental modification**.

$$Stigmergy = f(\text{Agent Action}, \text{Environmental Trace}, \text{Time})$$

In ants, stigmergy is realised through **chemical pheromones** — a volatile substance deposited on the ground that evaporates over time and is detected by chemoreceptors on the ant's antennae.

## 2.3 Ant Colonies — Real Ant Foraging Behaviour

Real ants are **almost blind**; they navigate almost entirely by **sensing chemical gradients**. Key ethological facts:

- **Species studied:** *Lasius niger*, *Iridomyrmex humilis* (Argentine ant), *Linepithema humile*.
- **Pheromone perception threshold:** Ants detect pheromone concentrations as low as $\mathbf{10^{-12} \text{ g/cm}}$ of trail.
- **Trail-following probability** is proportional to pheromone concentration following a **power law**.

## 2.4 The Double Bridge Experiment (Deneubourg, Aron, Goss, Pasteels, 1990)

This is the **most important KTU experiment** in Module 2. It established the empirical basis for ACO.

### Experimental Setup
A nest of ants is connected to a food source via **two bridges** of **unequal length** (short bridge S, long bridge L). Initially, no pheromone exists. Ants must choose randomly at first.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Result:** After a transient phase, the colony converges to using the **shorter bridge** with probability approaching 1. This is the empirical proof that a swarm can solve a *shortest-path* problem using only **local, indirect, chemical communication**.

### Deneubourg's Probabilistic Model (1990)

The probability $P_i$ that an ant chooses bridge $i$ is given by:

$$P_i = \frac{(k + p_i)^{\alpha}}{(k + p_i)^{\alpha} + (k + p_j)^{\alpha}}$$

$$P_j = 1 - P_i$$

Where:
- $p_i, p_j$ = Pheromone concentration on bridges $i$ and $j$ respectively
- $\alpha \geq 1$ = **Non-linearity parameter** (steepness of decision)
- $k$ = **Attractiveness constant** (degree of inherent bias/exploration); $k > 0$
- $i, j$ = The two candidate bridges

**Physical Constants/Empirical Values:**
- $\alpha \approx 2$ (for *Lasius niger* — empirically fitted)
- $k \approx 20$ (experiments by Deneubourg et al.)
- Pheromone evaporation half-life: $\sim$ **30–60 minutes** in laboratory conditions

## 2.5 Towards Artificial Ants (The Conceptual Bridge)

The transition from biological ants to artificial ants involves **four idealisations**:

| Biological Property | Artificial Ant Equivalent |
|--------------------|---------------------------|
| Real pheromone trail | Numerical pheromone matrix $\tau_{ij}$ |
| Random walk | Probabilistic transition rule |
| Time-dependent evaporation | Evaporation coefficient $\rho \in (0,1]$ |
| Concurrent movement | Sequential/Synchronous update |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** "An **artificial ant** is a stochastic constructive procedure that builds a solution by adding solution components to a partial solution, guided by a pheromone model and a heuristic model, while depositing pheromone on the components it has used." — Adapted from **Dorigo, Maniezzo & Colorni (1996)**.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Boundary / Units** |
|-------|-------------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $P_i = \dfrac{(k+p_i)^\alpha}{(k+p_i)^\alpha + (k+p_j)^\alpha}$ | Deneubourg bridge-choice probability | Dimensionless, $\alpha \geq 1$ |
| 2 | $\Delta p_i = \dfrac{Q}{L_i}$ | Pheromone deposit per ant (Dorigo) | $Q$ = constant, $L_i$ = path length |
| 3 | $p_i(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,p_i(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m}\Delta p_i^{(k)}$ | Pheromone update (evaporation + deposit) | $\rho \in [0,1]$, dimensionless |
| 4 | $p_i(t+1) = p_i(t) + \gamma \cdot p_i(t)(P_i - p_i^*)$ | Deneubourg continuous model (PEM) | $\gamma$ = learning rate |
| 5 | $P_{ij}^k(t) = \dfrac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^\beta}{\sum_{l \in N_i^k} [\tau_{il}(t)]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{il}]^\beta}$ | ACO transition rule | $\alpha, \beta$ = weight parameters |
| 6 | $\eta_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{d_{ij}}$ | Heuristic visibility (TSP) | $d_{ij}$ = distance |
| 7 | $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m}\Delta\tau_{ij}^k$ | Global pheromone update | $\rho \in (0,1)$ |
| 8 | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^k = \begin{cases} Q/L_k & \text{if ant } k \text{ uses } (i,j) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Ant System deposit rule | Ant-density model |
| 9 | $m$ | Number of artificial ants | $m \approx n$ (number of cities) |
| 10 | $NC_{max}$ | Max iterations (termination) | Tuning parameter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Anti-Break Rule:** The vertical bar `|` has been replaced with `\vert` in formulas to preserve markdown table integrity. Example: $\vert x \vert$ instead of $|x|$.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility of Ant-Based Optimisation

- **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** — Dorigo's original benchmark
- **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)** — Real-world logistics (e.g., Amazon, FedEx)
- **Network Routing** — AntNet for telecommunication packet routing
- **Job-Shop Scheduling** — Manufacturing optimisation
- **Protein Folding** — Bioinformatics
- **Graph Colouring** — Combinatorics

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Deneubourg's Bridge Choice Probability

### Step 1: Define the Setup
Consider a colony of $m$ ants at the bifurcation point. Two bridges $B_S$ (short) and $B_L$ (long) lead to the food source. At time $t=0$, no pheromone is present on either bridge.

### Step 2: Empirical Observation
It is observed experimentally that the probability of choosing a bridge follows a **non-linear function** of pheromone density. Let:
- $p_S(t)$ = pheromone on short bridge at time $t$
- $p_L(t)$ = pheromone on long bridge at time $t$

### Step 3: Assume Power-Law Response
From Weber-Fechner-like psychophysical laws, ant response is logarithmic/non-linear. A power-law is assumed:

$$P_S \propto p_S^{\alpha}, \quad P_L \propto p_L^{\alpha}$$

### Step 4: Normalise the Probabilities
Probabilities must sum to 1, so we introduce the attractiveness constant $k$ to prevent division by zero at $t=0$:

$$P_S = \frac{(k + p_S)^{\alpha}}{(k + p_S)^{\alpha} + (k + p_L)^{\alpha}}$$

### Step 5: Verify the Initial Condition
At $t=0$, $p_S = p_L = 0$:

$$P_S(0) = \frac{k^\alpha}{k^\alpha + k^\alpha} = \frac{k^\alpha}{2k^\alpha} = \frac{1}{2}$$

Thus, **initially, ants choose each bridge with equal probability 0.5**, which is consistent with experimental observation. ✓

### Step 6: Verify the Asymptotic Behaviour
Suppose a stochastic fluctuation causes $p_S > p_L$ slightly. Then:

$$P_S = \frac{(k + p_S)^{\alpha}}{(k + p_S)^{\alpha} + (k + p_L)^{\alpha}} > \frac{1}{2}$$

This positive deviation reinforces itself → **autocatalytic amplification**. Hence, the **shorter path is reinforced** more (shorter return time → more ants per unit time on short bridge). The system converges to:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} P_S \to 1, \quad \lim_{t \to \infty} P_L \to 0$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Pheromone Evaporation-Deposit Update (Ant System)

### Step 1: Define the Pheromone Matrix
Let $\tau_{ij}(t)$ be the pheromone intensity on edge $(i,j)$ at iteration $t$.

### Step 2: Apply Evaporation
A fraction $\rho$ of pheromone evaporates each iteration (negative feedback):

$$\tau_{ij}^{evap}(t+1) = (1 - \rho)\tau_{ij}(t)$$

### Step 3: Add Deposits
Each ant $k \in \{1, 2, \ldots, m\}$ deposits pheromone on edges it traversed:

$$\Delta\tau_{ij}^k = \begin{cases} Q/L_k & \text{if ant } k \text{ traversed edge } (i,j) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Where $L_k$ is the tour length of ant $k$ and $Q$ is a tunable constant (pheromone quantity per ant).

### Step 4: Combine
Total update rule:

$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m}\Delta\tau_{ij}^k$$

### Step 5: Verify the Steady-State
If a tour of constant length $L^*$ is found repeatedly by all $m$ ants, at steady state $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = \tau_{ij}(t) = \tau_{ij}^*$:

$$\tau_{ij}^* = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}^{*} + \frac{mQ}{L^*}$$

$$\rho \cdot \tau_{ij}^{*} = \frac{mQ}{L^*}$$

$$\tau_{ij}^{*} = \frac{mQ}{\rho L^*}$$

**Interpretation:** Pheromone at equilibrium is **inversely proportional to tour length** — shorter tours get higher pheromone, as expected. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation: ACO Transition Probability (Dorigo's Rule)

For ant $k$ at node $i$, the probability of choosing next node $j$ from the feasible set $N_i^k$:

$$P_{ij}^k(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^\beta}{\sum_{l \in N_i^k} [\tau_{il}(t)]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{il}]^\beta}$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

**Step 1:** Identify two information sources for an ant at node $i$:
- $\tau_{ij}$ — **A posteriori** knowledge (what previous ants "said" via pheromone)
- $\eta_{ij}$ — **A priori** knowledge (e.g., $1/d_{ij}$ for TSP; closer = better)

**Step 2:** Combine via weighted product (multiplicative aggregation):

$$\text{Desirability}_{ij} = \tau_{ij}^\alpha \cdot \eta_{ij}^\beta$$

**Step 3:** Apply weights $\alpha$ (pheromone weight) and $\beta$ (heuristic weight) to allow bias control:
- $\alpha = 0$ → pure heuristic (greedy nearest-neighbour)
- $\beta = 0$ → pure pheromone (stagnation-prone)

**Step 4:** Normalise so that $\sum_{j} P_{ij}^k = 1$ (valid probability distribution).

**Step 5:** Practical parameter ranges (Dorigo's recommendations):
- $\alpha \in [1, 2]$
- $\beta \in [2, 5]$
- $\rho \in [0.1, 0.5]$
- $m = n$ (number of ants = number of cities)
- $Q \in [1, 10000]$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Ant Models

| **Property** | **Ant System (AS)** | **Ant Colony System (ACS)** | **Max-Min Ant System (MMAS)** |
|--------------|--------------------|-----------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Pheromone deposit | All ants (global) | Best-so-far only | Best-so-far only |
| Pheromone bounds | $[0, \infty)$ | $[\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$ | $[\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$ |
| Transition rule | Proportional (Eq. 5) | Pseudo-random proportional | Proportional |
| Initial pheromone | $\tau_0$ | $\tau_0$ | $\tau_{max}$ |
| Local update | No | Yes (on traversal) | No |
| Stagnation handling | Poor | Better | Best (bound enforced) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** When asked to "compare" ACO variants, this matrix is your **gold standard answer**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]
**Define Swarm Intelligence. List any four properties of a swarm-intelligent system.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Swarm Intelligence (SI) is the discipline that studies the collective behaviour of decentralised, self-organised systems composed of many simple agents interacting locally with each other and their environment, leading to the emergence of intelligent global patterns (Bonabeau, Dorigo & Theraulaz, 1999).

**Four Properties:**
1. **Self-Organisation** — Global order emerges from local interactions without external control.
2. **Stigmergy** — Indirect communication through environmental modifications (e.g., pheromone).
3. **Division of Labour** — Specialised roles emerge dynamically based on task demand.
4. **Decentralised Control** — No single master agent; the system is fault-tolerant and robust.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention **"decentralised"** explicitly. Writing just "self-organised" loses 1 mark. The 2024 KTU marking scheme awards 1 mark for the definition and 0.5 marks per property × 4 = 2 marks.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**What is Stigmergy? Explain with an example from ant colonies.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Stigmergy is a mechanism of indirect coordination between agents in which the trace left by an action in a medium stimulates the performance of subsequent actions by the same or different agents. The term was coined by **Pierre-Paul Grassé (1959)** in the context of termite nest building.

**Example (Ant Colony):**
When a foraging ant finds food, it returns to the nest depositing a chemical substance called **pheromone** on the ground. Other ants detect this pheromone via their antennae and preferentially follow the trail. The stronger the trail (more ants have reinforced it), the more likely it is to be followed. The pheromone evaporates over time, which allows the colony to forget old, suboptimal trails. Thus, **the pheromone trail is the environmental trace**, and the ant's behaviour is **indirectly coordinated** by this trace — a textbook example of stigmergy.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "stigmergy = communication" — this is **too vague**. Always state that it is **indirect** communication **mediated by the environment** and cite Grassé (1959) for full 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]

**a) Explain the biological foundations of Swarm Intelligence. Discuss the four mechanisms of self-organisation with examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Biological Foundations:**

Swarm Intelligence is inspired by the observation that **simple individuals** (ants, bees, birds, fish, termites) following **simple local rules** can collectively exhibit **complex, intelligent global behaviour** without any central coordinator. The biological foundation rests on three pillars:
- **Decentralisation** — no leader
- **Local sensing** — agents perceive only nearby environment
- **Emergence** — global patterns not explicitly programmed

Examples include ant pheromone trails, bee waggle dances, bird flocking V-formations, and fish schooling evasion manoeuvres.

**(ii) Four Mechanisms of Self-Organisation (Bonabeau et al.):**

**1. Positive Feedback:** Reinforcement of successful structures. Example: ants deposit more pheromone on shorter, food-rich paths, attracting more ants (autocatalytic effect).

**2. Negative Feedback:** Dissolution/counter-balancing of structures to prevent runaway reinforcement. Example: pheromone **evaporation** prevents the colony from permanently locking onto an obsolete path.

**3. Randomness / Fluctuations:** Stochastic perturbations that allow exploration of new solutions. Example: an ant's random turn at a bifurcation enables discovery of new food sources.

**4. Multiple Interactions:** Amplification of microscopic signals into macroscopic patterns through many agents. Example: tens of thousands of ants collectively form a robust trail that no single ant could maintain alone.

**Conclusion:** These four mechanisms interact to balance **exploitation** (use known good solutions) and **exploration** (find new solutions), a key trade-off in any optimisation algorithm.

---

**b) Describe the Double Bridge Experiment by Deneubourg et al. (1990). Derive the bridge-choice probability formula. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Experimental Setup:**

Researchers (Deneubourg, Aron, Goss, Pasteels, 1990) connected an ant colony (*Linepithema humile*) to a food source using **two bridges of unequal length** (short bridge $S$ and long bridge $L$). Initially, no pheromone was present on either bridge.

**(ii) Observed Behaviour:**
- During the **transient phase**, ants chose either bridge with **equal probability (≈ 0.5)**.
- Gradually, the **short bridge** was reinforced more (because ants over it returned faster, depositing pheromone more frequently per unit time).
- After sufficient time, **almost all ants used the short bridge**.

**(iii) Derivation of the Probability Formula:**

**Step 1:** Empirical data showed that the ant's choice probability follows a non-linear (power-law) function of pheromone density:
$$P_S \propto (k + p_S)^{\alpha}, \quad P_L \propto (k + p_L)^{\alpha}$$

**Step 2:** Normalising such that $P_S + P_L = 1$:
$$P_S = \frac{(k + p_S)^{\alpha}}{(k + p_S)^{\alpha} + (k + p_L)^{\alpha}}$$

**Step 3:** Verify initial condition at $t=0$ (no pheromone, $p_S = p_L = 0$):
$$P_S(0) = \frac{k^{\alpha}}{k^{\alpha} + k^{\alpha}} = \frac{1}{2} \checkmark$$

**Step 4:** Verify asymptotic behaviour — once $p_S > p_L$, the positive feedback amplifies this difference, driving $P_S \to 1$ (shorter bridge wins).

**Step 5:** Constants: For *Lasius niger*, $\alpha \approx 2$, $k \approx 20$.

**Conclusion:** The experiment proved that **simple local rules + indirect communication = shortest-path solution**, providing the empirical foundation for **Ant Colony Optimisation** proposed by Dorigo in 1992.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]

**a) Discuss the transition from biological ants to artificial ants. What idealisations are introduced? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction:** The transition from real (biological) ants to **artificial ants** was accomplished by **Marco Dorigo in his PhD thesis (1992)**, giving birth to **Ant System (AS)** — the first ACO algorithm.

**Key Idealisations:**

1. **Discrete Environment:** Real ants live in a continuous 2D/3D world; artificial ants move on a **discrete graph** (e.g., cities of TSP).

2. **Memory of Path (Tabu List):** Real ants have limited memory; artificial ants maintain a **tabu list** of visited nodes to forbid revisits.

3. **Pheromone as a Numerical Variable:** Real pheromone is a chemical; artificial ants use a **pheromone matrix** $\tau_{ij} \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$.

4. **Deterministic Time Steps:** Real ants act asynchronously and continuously; artificial ants work in **synchronous iterations** $t = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$

5. **Reinforcement by Tour Quality:** Real pheromone deposit is roughly uniform; artificial ants deposit pheromone in **proportion to inverse tour length** $\Delta\tau = Q/L$.

6. **Evaporation as a Parameter:** Real evaporation is governed by chemistry; artificial ants use a tunable **evaporation rate** $\rho \in (0,1)$.

7. **Visibility Heuristic:** Real ants cannot "see" distance; artificial ants use a **heuristic** $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$.

**Differences Preserved:** Despite idealisations, the **two core ant behaviours** are preserved:
- Trail-following (proportional to pheromone)
- Trail-laying (deposit pheromone proportional to success)

**Conclusion:** This abstraction makes the model computationally tractable while preserving the **emergent self-organisation** property.

---

**b) Explain the Ant Colony Optimisation (ACO) algorithm with the pheromone update rule. State the transition probability rule used by artificial ants. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**ACO Algorithm (Ant System Variant):**

**Step 1 — Initialisation:** Set $\tau_{ij}(0) = \tau_0$ (small constant) for all edges; place $m$ ants randomly on $n$ nodes.

**Step 2 — Tour Construction:** Each ant $k$ at node $i$ selects the next node $j$ from feasible set $N_i^k$ with probability:

$$P_{ij}^k(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in N_i^k} [\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}$$

where $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ is the heuristic visibility.

**Step 3 — Pheromone Evaporation:**
$$\tau_{ij}(t) \leftarrow (1-\rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t)$$

**Step 4 — Pheromone Deposit:** Each ant $k$ deposits:
$$\Delta\tau_{ij}^k = \begin{cases} Q/L_k & \text{if edge } (i,j) \text{ in ant } k\text{'s tour} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Update:**
$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^k$$

**Step 6 — Termination:** Repeat Steps 2–5 until $t = NC_{max}$ or stagnation.

**Parameter Recommendations (Dorigo):**
- $m \approx n$ (ants = cities)
- $\alpha \in [1,2]$, $\beta \in [2,5]$, $\rho \in [0.1, 0.5]$, $Q = 100$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **AS (Ant System)** with **ACS (Ant Colony System)**. In AS, **all ants** deposit pheromone globally; in ACS, **only the best-so-far ant** deposits. Mixing these in your answer loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Module 2: Swarm Intelligence & Ant Colonies**

- ✅ **Swarm Intelligence (SI)** = Collective behaviour of decentralised, self-organised agents; coined by **Bonabeau, Dorigo & Theraulaz (1999)**.
- ✅ **Four Properties:** Self-organisation, Stigmergy, Division of Labour, Decentralised Control.
- ✅ **Four Mechanisms of Self-Org:** Positive Feedback, Negative Feedback, Randomness, Multiple Interactions.
- ✅ **Stigmergy** = Indirect, environment-mediated coordination; coined by **Grassé (1959)** for termites.
- ✅ **Deneubourg's Double Bridge (1990)** = Foundational experiment using *Linepithema humile*; proved shortest-path emergence.
- ✅ **Deneubourg's Formula:** $P_i = \frac{(k+p_i)^{\alpha}}{(k+p_i)^{\alpha} + (k+p_j)^{\alpha}}$; parameters $\alpha \approx 2$, $k \approx 20$.
- ✅ **First ACO Algorithm** = **Ant System (AS)** by **Marco Dorigo (1992 PhD thesis)**.
- ✅ **ACO Transition Rule:** $P_{ij}^k = \frac{\tau_{ij}^{\alpha} \cdot \eta_{ij}^{\beta}}{\sum_{l} \tau_{il}^{\alpha} \cdot \eta_{il}^{\beta}}$; visibility $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$.
- ✅ **Pheromone Update:** $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_k \Delta\tau_{ij}^k$; deposit $\Delta\tau = Q/L_k$.
- ✅ **Equilibrium Pheromone:** $\tau_{ij}^{*} = \frac{mQ}{\rho L^{*}}$ — inversely proportional to tour length.
- ✅ **Parameter Ranges:** $\alpha \in [1,2]$, $\beta \in [2,5]$, $\rho \in [0.1, 0.5]$, $m \approx n$.
- ✅ **Real vs Artificial Ants:** Real ants are blind, use chemical pheromone, no memory of visited nodes; artificial ants have memory (tabu list), numerical pheromone, and use heuristic visibility.
- ✅ **Trade-off:** Positive feedback (exploitation) ↔ Negative feedback via evaporation (exploration) — must be balanced.
- ✅ **ACO Variants:** AS (1992), ACS (1997), MMAS (2000); MMAS is best for stagnation handling.
- ✅ **Applications:** TSP, VRP, Network Routing (AntNet), Job-Shop Scheduling, Graph Colouring, Bioinformatics.

# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** is a **population-based metaheuristic** inspired by the foraging behavior of real ants, proposed by **Marco Dorigo** in his PhD thesis (1991, Politecnico di Milano) and formally published in 1996. ACO is used to solve **combinatorial optimization problems (COPs)** by employing a colony of artificial ants that construct candidate solutions stochastically and communicate indirectly via a **pheromone model**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, ACO is positioned as a **constructive metaheuristic** (it builds solutions component by component) belonging to the broader family of **nature-inspired swarm intelligence algorithms**.

**Standard notation used in KTU reference texts:**

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $m$ | Number of artificial ants in the colony |
| $n$ | Number of nodes/cities/components |
| $\tau_{ij}$ | Pheromone trail intensity on edge $(i, j)$ |
| $\eta_{ij}$ | Heuristic desirability (visibility) of edge $(i, j)$ |
| $\alpha$ | Pheromone influence exponent |
| $\beta$ | Heuristic information influence exponent |
| $\rho$ | Pheromone evaporation rate ($0 < \rho < 1$) |
| $L_k$ | Length of tour constructed by ant $k$ |
| $p_{ij}^k$ | Probability of ant $k$ moving from $i$ to $j$ |

---

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — How Ants Solve Problems Without a Map

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you spill sugar on the kitchen floor. Ants discover it via random wandering. The first ant that finds sugar returns to the nest **laying down a chemical trail (pheromone)**. Other ants follow the strongest trail — they don't *see* the sugar, they *smell* the chemical. As more ants traverse the shortest path, pheromone accumulates **faster** (shorter path → more return trips per unit time → more pheromone deposited per unit length). Longer paths have pheromone that **evaporates** before being reinforced. Eventually, the entire colony converges on the **shortest path** — an emergent, decentralized solution to a shortest-path problem.

**The three biological principles abstracted into ACO:**

1. **Stigmergy** — Indirect communication via environment modification (pheromone).
2. **Stochastic constructive search** — Each ant builds a solution probabilistically, not deterministically.
3. **Shortest-path discovery with positive feedback** — Good solutions are reinforced; bad ones decay.

---

## 1.3 Combinatorial Optimization (COP) — The Problem Class ACO Solves

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combinatorial Optimization** is the class of optimization problems where the solution space is a **finite (or countably infinite) set of discrete structures**, and the goal is to find the **optimal** (minimum or maximum) solution with respect to a discrete objective function.

**Formal definition:** Given a finite set $\mathcal{S}$ of feasible solutions and an objective function $f: \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_0^+$, a COP is:

$$
s^{*} = \arg\min_{s \in \mathcal{S}} f(s)
$$

**Classic COPs solvable by ACO:**

| Problem | Domain | Objective |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **TSP** (Traveling Salesman Problem) | Routing | Minimize total tour length |
| **QAP** (Quadratic Assignment Problem) | Facility layout | Minimize flow × distance cost |
| **VRP** (Vehicle Routing Problem) | Logistics | Minimize total route cost |
| **JSSP** (Job Shop Scheduling) | Manufacturing | Minimize makespan |
| **GCP** (Graph Coloring) | Networks | Minimize colors used |
| **SCP** (Set Covering Problem) | Resource allocation | Minimize sets selected |
| **BPP** (Bin Packing) | Cutting stock | Minimize bins used |

> [!NOTE]
> Most real-world COPs tackled by ACO are **NP-hard**, meaning exact algorithms scale exponentially. ACO provides **near-optimal solutions in polynomial time**, which is the engineering trade-off of interest to KTU examiners.

---

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of ACO — S-ACO, Metaheuristic ACO, ACO Algorithms

The KTU 2024 syllabus organises ACO into a **progression from simple to advanced**:

**Pillar 1 — S-ACO (Simple / Ant System):** The original, foundational ACO algorithm. Pure ant-based constructive search. Easiest to analyze mathematically, easiest for exam questions.

**Pillar 2 — ACO Metaheuristic:** A general algorithmic *framework* (paradigm) into which variants (ACS, MMAS, AS-rank, Elitist AS) are plugged. Introduced by Dorigo & Di Caro (1999) to unify all ant-based algorithms.

**Pillar 3 — ACO Metaheuristic Problem Solving:** Application of the metaheuristic to specific COPs — TSP, VRP, QAP, JSSP — and integration with **local search** for hybrid performance.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU board exam purposes: *S-ACO* = exam-safe basic version. *ACO Metaheuristic* = framework + variants. *Problem Solving* = application. *Local Search* = enhancement. *Scope* = research/industry context.

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 S-ACO (Simple Ant Colony Optimization / Ant System)

S-ACO is the **simplest** ACO variant, comprising three algorithmic phases that repeat iteratively:

### 2.1.1 Phase 1 — Solution Construction (Transition Rule)

Each ant $k$ currently at node $i$ chooses the next node $j$ probabilistically:

$$
p_{ij}^{k}(t) = \frac{\left[\tau_{ij}(t)\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{ij}\right]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_{i}^{k}} \left[\tau_{il}(t)\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{il}\right]^{\beta}}, \quad j \in \mathcal{N}_{i}^{k}
$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{N}_{i}^{k}$ = set of unvisited neighbors of ant $k$ at node $i$
- $\alpha \geq 0$ controls **pheromone importance** (exploitation of past experience)
- $\beta \geq 0$ controls **heuristic importance** (greedy bias toward promising moves)
- $\eta_{ij} = \frac{1}{d_{ij}}$ for TSP (inverse of distance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When $\alpha = 0$, S-ACO reduces to a **stochastic greedy algorithm**. When $\beta = 0$, only pheromone matters — convergence becomes slow and erratic. The standard setting is $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 2$ to $\beta = 5$.

### 2.1.2 Phase 2 — Pheromone Evaporation (Global Decay)

To prevent premature convergence and allow exploration:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t) \leftarrow (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t), \quad \forall (i, j) \in E
$$

Where $\rho \in (0, 1]$ is the **evaporation coefficient**. Typical value: $\rho = 0.5$.

### 2.1.3 Phase 3 — Pheromone Deposit (Reinforcement)

Each ant $k$ deposits pheromone proportional to the **quality** of its tour:

$$
\Delta \tau_{ij}^{k} = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{L_k(t)} & \text{if edge } (i, j) \text{ belongs to ant } k\text{'s tour} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}
$$

The complete S-ACO update rule is:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** In S-ACO (Ant System), **all** ants deposit pheromone. In ACS (Ant Colony System), **only the best ant** deposits. This single change dramatically alters performance.

---

## 2.2 ACO Metaheuristic — The General Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **ACO Metaheuristic** is **not a single algorithm** — it is a **generalized algorithmic template** (a paradigm) within which specific ACO variants are instantiated. It was formalized by Dorigo, Di Caro, & Gambardella (1999) in the *Artificial Life* journal and refined in the *IEEE Computational Intelligence Magazine* (2006).

The metaheuristic extends S-ACO with three additional mechanisms:

### 2.2.1 The ACO Metaheuristic Algorithm (Pseudocode-style, Mathematical)

**Step 1 — Initialization:**
Set pheromone trails: $\tau_{ij}(0) = \tau_0 > 0$ (often $\tau_0 = \frac{m}{C^{nn}}$, where $C^{nn}$ is nearest-neighbour tour length).

**Step 2 — Schedule Activities:**
The three activities are scheduled, possibly concurrently:
- (a) AntDaemonAction() — optional centralized actions (e.g., local search)
- (b) ConstructSolutions() — ants build solutions
- (c) UpdatePheromones() — evaporation + deposit

**Step 3 — ConstructSolutions():**
Each ant $k$ applies the **state transition rule**. ACS introduces the **pseudo-random proportional rule**:

$$
j = \begin{cases} \arg\max_{l \in \mathcal{N}_{i}^{k}} \left\{ [\tau_{il}]^{\alpha} [\eta_{il}]^{\beta} \right\} & \text{if } q \leq q_0 \text{ (exploitation)} \\ J & \text{otherwise (exploration)} \end{cases}
$$

Where $q \sim U(0,1)$ and $q_0 \in [0,1]$ is a tunable parameter, and $J$ is sampled by the S-ACO probability rule.

**Step 4 — UpdatePheromones():**
For ACS, only the iteration-best or global-best ant deposits:

$$
\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij} + \rho \cdot \Delta \tau_{ij}^{best}
$$

with $\Delta \tau_{ij}^{best} = \frac{1}{L_{best}}$.

**Step 5 — Local Pheromone Update (ACS-specific):**
After each move in ACS, apply **online local update** to encourage exploration:

$$
\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1 - \xi) \cdot \tau_{ij} + \xi \cdot \tau_0
$$

where $\xi \in (0,1]$ is the local decay coefficient.

### 2.2.2 Variants of the ACO Metaheuristic (High-Yield Table)

| Variant | Year | Key Distinguishing Feature | Pheromone Deposit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Ant System (AS)** / S-ACO | 1991 | All ants deposit | All ants |
| **Elitist AS** | 1992 | Best-so-far ant gets bonus | All + $e \cdot \tau_{ij}^{bs}$ bonus |
| **Rank-based AS (AS-rank)** | 1997 | Only top $(w-1)$ ranked ants deposit, weighted by rank | Rank-weighted |
| **Max-Min AS (MMAS)** | 2000 | $\tau_{ij}$ clamped to $[\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$ | Best-so-far / iteration-best only |
| **Ant Colony System (ACS)** | 1997 | Pseudo-random rule + local update | Iteration-best only |

---

## 2.3 Local Search Methods (Hybrid ACO)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pure ACO is a **construction heuristic** — it builds solutions from scratch. Adding a **local search** phase dramatically improves solution quality. Modern high-performance ACO = **ACO metaheuristic + local search = hybrid metaheuristic**. This is the **de facto standard** in research.

### 2.3.1 Why Local Search?

ACO explores the **global search space** efficiently but produces solutions that are locally improvable. Local search **exploits** neighborhoods.

### 2.3.2 Common Local Search Operators for TSP

| Operator | Type | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **2-opt** | Intra-tour swap | Remove 2 edges, reconnect crossing-free |
| **3-opt** | Intra-tour swap | Remove 3 edges, try 7 reconnections (excludes 2-opt subset) |
| **Or-opt** | Intra-tour relocation | Move a chain of 1–3 cities to another position |
| **LK (Lin-Kernighan)** | Variable k-opt | Adaptive, considered gold standard for TSP |
| **Node-swap** | Inter/intra | Exchange two node positions |

### 2.3.3 The 2-opt Local Search (Mathematical)

For a tour $\pi = (\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots, \pi_n)$, find $i < j$ such that the new tour $\pi'$ obtained by reversing segment $[\pi_{i+1}, \ldots, \pi_j]$ yields:

$$
d(\pi_i, \pi_{i+1}) + d(\pi_j, \pi_{j+1}) > d(\pi_i, \pi_j) + d(\pi_{i+1}, \pi_{j+1})
$$

If true, accept the improvement (greedy 2-opt).

---

| 9 | $j = \arg\max_{l} \{[\tau_{il}]^{\alpha} [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}\}$ if $q \leq q_0$ | ACS pseudo-random rule | $q_0 \in [0,1]$ |
| 10 | $L_{best} = \min_{k \in \{1,\ldots,m\}} L_k$ | Best tour in iteration | — |
| 11 | $L_{gb} = \min_{t \in \{1,\ldots,T\}} L_{best}(t)$ | Global best tour so far | $T$ = total iterations |
| 12 | $p = 1 - (1 - \rho)^t$ | Pheromone decay probability after $t$ steps | — |
| 13 | $\bar{L} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{k=1}^{m} L_k$ | Mean tour length | — |
| 14 | $CV = \frac{\sigma_L}{\bar{L}}$ | Coefficient of variation (convergence metric) | $\sigma_L$ = std. dev. |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> ACO is a **production-grade optimizer** used in industries served by KTU placements:
> - **Logistics & Supply Chain** (Amazon, Flipkart): VRP, last-mile delivery.
> - **Telecommunications** (BSNL, Jio, Airtel): Network routing, MPLS path optimization.
> - **VLSI Design** (Intel, Texas Instruments): Wire routing, gate placement.
> - **Manufacturing** (TCS, Bosch): Job-shop scheduling, robotic cell flow.
> - **Bioinformatics** (IISc labs): Protein structure prediction, DNA sequencing.

The key engineering insight: **ACO balances exploration and exploitation** better than pure genetic algorithms for **graph-structured problems** (routing, scheduling, assignment).

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: S-ACO Pheromone Update from First Principles

### 3.1.1 Starting Premise — Why a Decay + Deposit Model?

Real pheromone in nature:
1. **Evaporates** exponentially with time constant $\tau_{evap}$.
2. Is **deposited** in discrete amounts by each ant that traverses an edge.

We model this as a **discrete-time stochastic process** on a graph $G = (V, E)$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1 — Continuous decay model.** Let $c(t)$ be pheromone concentration on an edge at time $t$. Evaporation gives:

$$
\frac{dc}{dt} = -\lambda c
$$

Solution: $c(t) = c(0) e^{-\lambda t}$.

**Step 2 — Discretize.** With $t \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$ and step size 1:

$$
c(t+1) = c(t) \cdot e^{-\lambda}
$$

Define $e^{-\lambda} \equiv (1 - \rho)$, where $\rho$ is the evaporation rate:

$$
c(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot c(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Add deposit term.** If $m$ ants traverse edge $(i, j)$ during iteration $t$, each ant $k$ adds $\Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}$:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}
$$

**Step 4 — Define deposit inversely proportional to tour quality.** For TSP, a shorter tour is better. Let $L_k$ = length of ant $k$'s tour. Define:

$$
\Delta \tau_{ij}^{k} = \begin{cases} \frac{Q}{L_k} & (i, j) \in T^{k} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

where $Q$ is a constant (often $Q = 1$).

**Step 5 — Final S-ACO update (Ant System):**

$$
\boxed{\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}}
$$

### 3.1.3 Convergence Interpretation

After $t$ iterations with no further deposits, the pheromone on an unused edge decays geometrically:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t) = \tau_{ij}(0) \cdot (1 - \rho)^{t}
$$

This justifies the need for **lower bounds** in MMAS — without clamping, $\tau_{ij} \to 0$ and the algorithm can stagnate.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — S-ACO on a 4-City TSP

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Setup:** 4 cities labelled A, B, C, D. Distance matrix $D$ (symmetric Euclidean):
>
> | From $\backslash$ To | A | B | C | D |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | **A** | 0 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
> | **B** | 2 | 0 | 5 | 1 |
> | **C** | 3 | 5 | 0 | 6 |
> | **D** | 4 | 1 | 6 | 0 |
>
> Parameters: $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 1$, $\rho = 0.1$, $m = 2$ ants, $Q = 1$, $\tau_{ij}(0) = 1$ for all edges.

### 3.2.1 Step A — Compute Heuristic Information $\eta_{ij}$

$$
\eta_{ij} = \frac{1}{d_{ij}}
$$

$$
\eta_{AB} = \tfrac{1}{2} = 0.5, \quad \eta_{AC} = \tfrac{1}{3} \approx 0.333, \quad \eta_{AD} = \tfrac{1}{4} = 0.25
$$

$$
\eta_{BC} = 0.2, \quad \eta_{BD} = 1, \quad \eta_{CD} = \tfrac{1}{6} \approx 0.167
$$

### 3.2.2 Step B — Ant 1 starts at A, chooses next city

**Allowed moves from A:** {B, C, D}.

Compute numerator $[\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha} [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta} = \tau_{ij} \cdot \eta_{ij}$ (since $\alpha = \beta = 1$):

$$
A \to B: 1 \cdot 0.5 = 0.5
$$

$$
A \to C: 1 \cdot 0.333 = 0.333
$$

$$
A \to D: 1 \cdot 0.25 = 0.25
$$

$$
\text{Sum} = 0.5 + 0.333 + 0.25 = 1.083
$$

Probabilities:

$$
p_{AB} = \frac{0.5}{1.083} \approx 0.462
$$

$$
p_{AC} = \frac{0.333}{1.083} \approx 0.308
$$

$$
p_{AD} = \frac{0.25}{1.083} \approx 0.231
$$

Suppose a random draw gives $r_1 = 0.40 \in [0, 0.462)$ → **Ant 1 moves A → B**.

### 3.2.3 Step C — Ant 1 at B, allowed moves {C, D}

$$
B \to C: 1 \cdot 0.2 = 0.2
$$

$$
B \to D: 1 \cdot 1 = 1.0
$$

$$
p_{BC} = \frac{0.2}{1.2} \approx 0.167, \quad p_{BD} = \frac{0.833}{1.2} \approx 0.833
$$

Random draw $r_2 = 0.50 \in [0.167, 1.0]$ → **Ant 1 moves B → D**.

### 3.2.4 Step D — Ant 1 at D, only {C} available

Forced move: **D → C**, then **C → A** (return).

**Ant 1 tour:** A → B → D → C → A, $L_1 = 2 + 1 + 6 + 3 = 12$.

### 3.2.5 Step E — Ant 2 (similar)

Suppose Ant 2 chooses: A → C → B → D → A, $L_2 = 3 + 5 + 1 + 4 = 13$.

### 3.2.6 Step F — Pheromone Update

Each ant deposits $\Delta \tau_{ij}^{k} = 1/L_k$ on its tour edges.

**Ant 1 (L₁ = 12):** Each of 4 edges gets $1/12 \approx 0.0833$.

**Ant 2 (L₂ = 13):** Each of 4 edges gets $1/13 \approx 0.0769$.

Update rule: $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow 0.9 \cdot \tau_{ij} + \Delta \tau_{Ant1} + \Delta \tau_{Ant2}$.

Example for edge **B–D** (used by both ants):

$$
\tau_{BD}(1) = 0.9 \cdot 1 + 0.0833 + 0.0769 = 0.9 + 0.1602 = 1.0602
$$

Example for edge **A–C** (used by Ant 2 only):

$$
\tau_{AC}(1) = 0.9 \cdot 1 + 0 + 0.0769 = 0.9769
$$

Example for unused edge **A–D**:

$$
\tau_{AD}(1) = 0.9 \cdot 1 + 0 + 0 = 0.9
$$

> [!TIP]
> Notice pheromone **accumulates on short-tour edges** (B–D, in the optimal 9-length tour A-B-D-C-A would be even shorter) and **decays on unused edges**. This is the constructive feedback mechanism in action.

### 3.2.7 Iteration 2 (sketched)

With updated $\tau$, recompute probabilities. The probability of choosing short edges increases — **positive feedback** drives convergence.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — 2-opt Local Search on a 4-City Tour

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Tour:** A → B → D → C → A, $L = 12$.
>
> **Try 2-opt between edges (A, B) and (D, C):**
> Reverse segment B → D → C to C → D → B.
> New tour: A → C → D → B → A.
> New length: $L' = d_{AC} + d_{CD} + d_{DB} + d_{BA} = 3 + 6 + 1 + 2 = 12$.
> No improvement.
>
> **Try 2-opt between (A, B) and (C, A):** Reverse B → D → C → A segment → not valid (closed loop).
>
> **Try 2-opt between (B, D) and (A, B):** Reverse D → C → A segment → tour: A → D → C → B → A, $L' = 4 + 6 + 5 + 2 = 17$. Worse.
>
> **Conclusion:** The 4-city tour A-B-D-C-A is **2-opt optimal** (which it is — the only shorter tour is A-B-D-C-A = 12 vs A-B-C-D-A = 16; actually A-B-D-C-A is optimal for this matrix).

This example illustrates that **local search guarantees local optimality** but not global optimality — motivating hybrid ACO + LS.

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 ACO Metaheuristic — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **complete data flow** of the ACO metaheuristic, including initialization, construction, pheromone update, and optional local search.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([START]) --> initNode["InitializeParameters: alpha, beta, rho, m, tau_0"]
    initNode --> seedNode["InitializePheromoneTrails: tau_ij = tau_0 for all edges"]
    seedNode --> iterNode["For t = 1 to T iterations"]

    iterNode --> constructNode["ConstructSolutionsPhase: m ants build tours"]
    constructNode --> transNode["Apply Transition Rule: probabilistic or pseudo-random"]
    transNode --> evalNode["Evaluate Tours: compute L_k for each ant"]
    evalNode --> lsNode{"Apply Local Search?"}

    lsNode -- Yes --> lsApply["2-opt / 3-opt / LK on each tour"]
    lsNode -- No --> updateNode
    lsApply --> updateNode["UpdatePheromones: evaporate then deposit"]

    updateNode --> evapNode["Evaporation: tau_ij = 1 - rho times tau_ij"]
    evapNode --> depositNode{"Which Ant Deposits?"}
    depositNode -- Ant System --> allAnts["All m ants deposit: 1 over L_k"]
    depositNode -- ACS --> bestAnt["Best ant only: rho times 1 over L_best"]
    depositNode -- MMAS --> mmAnt["Best ant with clamping to tau_min, tau_max"]
    allAnts --> statNode
    bestAnt --> statNode
    mmAnt --> statNode

    statNode["Statistics and Convergence Check"] --> convNode{"Converged or t = T?"}
    convNode -- No --> iterNode
    convNode -- Yes --> outNode["Output Global Best Tour L_gb"]

    outNode --> stopNode([END])

    classDef paramStyle fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef procStyle fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef decStyle fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000

    class initNode,seedNode paramStyle
    class constructNode,transNode,evalNode,evapNode,allAnts,bestAnt,mmAnt,statNode,outNode,lsApply procStyle

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nest["Ant Nest"] -- Random Walk --> food1["Food Source A: long path"]
    nest -- Random Walk --> food2["Food Source B: short path"]

    food1 -- Ant returns, lays pheromone --> evap1["Pheromone evaporates slowly"]
    food2 -- Ant returns, lays pheromone --> evap2["Pheromone accumulates fast short path"]

    evap1 --> choice["Next ant: follows strongest trail"]
    evap2 --> choice

    choice -- Strongest = short path --> converge["Colony converges on shortest path"]
    evap1 -.weak signal.-> choice
```

```mermaid
    acs --> note5["Pseudo-random proportional rule plus local update."]
    hyperCube --> note6["Pheromone normalized to 0,1 interval."]

    acoRoot --> hybrid["Hybrid ACO + Local Search"]
    hybrid --> hybridA["ACO + 2-opt"]
    hybrid --> hybridB["ACO + LK"]
    hybrid --> hybridC["ACO + 3-opt"]
```
