## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the Particle Swarm Optimization algorithm in detail. Discuss the role of inertia weight in balancing exploration and exploitation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. PSO Algorithm — Step-by-Step Procedure:**

PSO maintains a swarm of $N$ particles, each representing a candidate solution to the $D$-dimensional optimisation problem. Each particle $i$ is characterised by position $\vec{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^D$ and velocity $\vec{v}_i \in \mathbb{R}^D$.

**Initialisation (Step 0):** The positions and velocities are initialised randomly within the search space bounds $[x_{min}, x_{max}]$ and $[-V_{max}, V_{max}]$ respectively.

**Iterative Process (Step 1 onwards):** For each iteration $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T_{max}$, the following updates are executed for every particle $i$ and every dimension $d$:

**Velocity Update:**

$$v_{id}(t+1) = w \cdot v_{id}(t) + c_1 r_1 \left( p_{best,id} - x_{id}(t) \right) + c_2 r_2 \left( g_{best,d} - x_{id}(t) \right)$$

**Position Update:**

$$x_{id}(t+1) = x_{id}(t) + v_{id}(t+1)$$

**Personal Best Update:**

$$p_{best,i}(t+1) = \begin{cases} x_i(t+1) & \text{if } f(x_i(t+1)) < f(p_{best,i}(t)) \\ p_{best,i}(t) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Global Best Update:**

$$g_{best}(t+1) = \arg\min_{i=1..N} f(p_{best,i}(t+1))$$

**Termination:** The loop halts when $t = T_{max}$ or when $\vert f(g_{best}(t+1)) - f(g_{best}(t)) \vert < \epsilon$.

**2. Role of Inertia Weight $w$:**

The inertia weight, introduced by **Shi and Eberhart (1998)**, controls the influence of the previous velocity on the current velocity.

- **Large $w$ (e.g., 0.9):** The particle retains strong momentum from its previous motion, favouring **global exploration**. The swarm searches broadly across the search space but may not converge precisely.
- **Small $w$ (e.g., 0.4):** The particle forgets its past trajectory quickly, favouring **local exploitation**. The swarm refines solutions near the current best, but risks premature convergence.
- **Adaptive (Linearly Decreasing) $w$:** Starts at $w_{max} = 0.9$ and decreases to $w_{min} = 0.4$ over iterations — the most common KTU-recommended approach, implementing the **explore-then-exploit** philosophy.

The inertia weight thus mathematically tunes the **exploration-exploitation trade-off**, which is the central design challenge of every meta-heuristic. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of Particle Swarm Optimization. How does the position update mechanism help in finding the global optimum? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working of PSO (with diagram description):**

The PSO algorithm can be visualised as a flock of birds (particles) moving through a $D$-dimensional search space. The diagram in Section 4.1 (flowchart) summarises the iterative flow.

At every iteration, each particle $i$ calculates its new velocity as a **stochastic weighted sum of three trend vectors**:

1. **Inertia vector $w \vec{v}_i(t)$:** Preserves the particle's momentum — the direction and speed it had been travelling.
2. **Cognitive vector $c_1 r_1 (p_{best,i} - \vec{x}_i(t))$:** Pulls the particle towards the best position it has personally ever visited.
3. **Social vector $c_2 r_2 (g_{best} - \vec{x}_i(t))$:** Pulls the particle towards the best position discovered by the entire swarm.

The position is then advanced by the new velocity, akin to **Newton's first law of motion** ($x_{new} = x_{old} + v \cdot \Delta t$ with $\Delta t = 1$).

**How Position Update Aids Global Optimum Discovery:**

- The **inertia term** allows particles to overshoot local optima and continue searching — this prevents the swarm from being trapped in poor local minima.
- The **cognitive term** ensures each particle remembers and returns to its own promising regions, maintaining a degree of **individual exploration** so the swarm does not collapse prematurely.
- The **social term** transfers knowledge across the swarm — when one particle finds a better region, all others are rapidly attracted towards it. This **broadcast mechanism** is what makes PSO converge faster than Genetic Algorithms, which rely on slow generational crossover.
- The **random multipliers $r_1, r_2$** inject stochasticity, ensuring the search is not deterministic and that the swarm does not collapse into a single point.
- **Velocity clamping** by $V_{max}$ prevents particles from diverging out of the search space, maintaining numerical stability.

The combined effect is that the swarm exhibits **emergent intelligence** — collectively, the particles chart trajectories that spiral towards the global optimum even though no single particle "knows" where it lies. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the modifications required in standard PSO to handle **dynamic optimization problems**. Explain at least three change-detection and response strategies. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **dynamic optimisation problem (DOP)** is one in which the objective function, constraints, or the location of the optimum changes with time. Standard PSO assumes a static landscape, so it must be enhanced for DOPs.

**Three Core Modifications Required:**

**1. Change Detection Mechanism:**

A **sentinel / monitor particle** is re-evaluated every few iterations. If its fitness value deviates from its previously remembered value beyond a threshold $\theta$, the environment is declared to have changed.

$$\text{Change Detected} \iff \vert f_{sentinel}(t) - f_{sentinel}(t-1) \vert > \theta$$

Alternatively, the global best $g_{best}$ is monitored — a sudden change in $f(g_{best})$ signals a landscape shift.

**2. Diversity Re-Introduction Response:**

Upon change detection, the algorithm must **inject diversity** to escape the (now-stale) attractor. Strategies include:

- **Re-initialise worst particles:** Replace the bottom 10–20% of particles with random positions in the search space. This breaks the "swarm around old optimum" lock-in.
- **Re-evaluate ALL particles:** Mandatory, because old $p_{best}$ and $g_{best}$ values may be invalid in the new landscape.

**3. Memory-Based Multi-Swarm Strategy (Eberhart & Shi, 2001):**

- Maintain a **memory archive** of past optima locations. If the new optimum is near a previous one, particles can be **re-seeded** from the memory — this drastically reduces convergence time.
- Use **multiple sub-swarms**: one **convergent sub-swarm** refines the current optimum, while a **divergent sub-swarm** explores new regions.

**4. Charged/Quantum PSO:**

- In **Charged PSO (CPSO)**, particles repel each other at close range, maintaining diversity even during convergence.
- In **Quantum PSO (QPSO)**, particles are attracted by a quantum-delta potential well centred at $g_{best}$, which guarantees bounded exploration.

These modifications allow PSO to continuously track a moving optimum — a property called **dynamic tracking** or **optimisation in non-stationary environments**. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Explain the **constriction coefficient** approach in PSO. Derive the value of the constriction coefficient $\chi$ for the canonical parameter setting. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Constriction Coefficient Approach:**

The constriction coefficient method, proposed by **Clerc and Kennedy (2002)**, provides a mathematically rigorous alternative to the inertia weight for guaranteeing PSO convergence. The modified velocity update is:

$$v_{id}(t+1) = \chi \left[ v_{id}(t) + c_1 r_1 (p_{best,id} - x_{id}(t)) + c_2 r_2 (g_{best,d} - x_{id}(t)) \right]$$

where $\chi$ is the **constriction factor**.

**Derivation (simplified deterministic case, single particle, single dimension, $p_{best} = g_{best} = P$):**

Let $\phi = c_1 + c_2$. The simplified update is:

$$v(t+1) = \chi \left[ v(t) + \phi (P - x(t)) \right]$$
$$x(t+1) = x(t) + v(t+1)$$

Define $y(t) = P - x(t)$ (distance from attractor). Then:

$$y(t+1) = -v(t+1) = -\chi v(t) - \chi \phi y(t)$$

Also, $v(t+1) = -y(t+1)$, so:

$$v(t+1) = -(-\chi v(t) - \chi \phi y(t)) = \chi v(t) + \chi \phi y(t)$$

Wait — re-derive carefully. The position update is $x(t+1) = x(t) + v(t+1)$, so:

$$y(t+1) = P - x(t+1) = P - x(t) - v(t+1) = y(t) - v(t+1)$$

Substituting $v(t+1) = \chi v(t) + \chi \phi y(t)$:

$$y(t+1) = y(t) - \chi v(t) - \chi \phi y(t) = (1 - \chi \phi) y(t) - \chi v(t)$$

This is a coupled 2-D linear system in $(y, v)$. The characteristic equation is:

$$\lambda^2 - (1 - \chi \phi + \chi) \lambda + \chi = 0$$

Substituting $\chi = 1$ (boundary) and $\phi = 4$ (minimum for real roots):

$$\lambda^2 - (1 - 4 + 1) \lambda + 1 = \lambda^2 + 2\lambda + 1 = (\lambda + 1)^2 = 0$$

For the system to be **marginally stable** with $\chi < 1$ (proper convergence):

$$\chi = \frac{2}{\vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi^2 - 4\phi} \vert} = \frac{2}{\vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi(\phi - 4)} \vert}$$

**Canonical Value Computation:** For $\phi = c_1 + c_2 = 4.1$ (with $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$):

$$\chi = \frac{2}{\vert 2 - 4.1 - \sqrt{4.1 \cdot 0.1} \vert} = \frac{2}{\vert -2.1 - \sqrt{0.41} \vert} = \frac{2}{\vert -2.1 - 0.6403 \vert} = \frac{2}{2.7403} \approx 0.7298$$

**Therefore $\chi \approx 0.7298$.** The canonical settings $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$ and $\chi = 0.7298$ are equivalent to the **inertia weight settings $w = 0.7298$ and $c_1 = c_2 = 1.496$** — they describe the same convergent dynamics. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PSO Answers — Read Before You Write!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the velocity clamping step** — Most students write the velocity equation but omit the $V_{max}$ clamping. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Confusing $p_{best}$ and $g_{best}$** — $p_{best}$ is per-particle; $g_{best}$ is global. Writing them interchangeably loses marks.
> 3. **Using `c1 = c2 = 2` without deriving Clerc-Kennedy** — When asked for *constriction coefficient derivation*, you MUST show the characteristic equation and the discriminant condition $\phi > 4$.
> 4. **Forgetting to update $p_{best}$ for worsening particles** — If a particle moves to a worse position, $p_{best}$ stays the same. Many students erroneously "update" $p_{best}$ to the new worse position.
> 5. **Dynamic PSO answers without mention of sentinel/monitor particle** — Change detection is a mandatory part of any DOP answer; omitting it loses 2–3 marks.
> 6. **No diagram in 14-mark answers** — KTU examiners specifically allocate marks for *neat labelled diagrams* of algorithm flow. Always include the flowchart.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — PSO**

**Core Concepts:**
- [ ] PSO = population-based meta-heuristic inspired by social behaviour of bird flocks / fish schools.
- [ ] Proposed by **Kennedy and Eberhart (1995)**; inertia weight by **Shi and Eberhart (1998)**; constriction by **Clerc and Kennedy (2002)**.
- [ ] Each particle has position $\vec{x}_i$, velocity $\vec{v}_i$, memory $p_{best,i}$, and access to $g_{best}$.

**Equations to Memorise Verbatim:**
- [ ] Velocity: $v_{id}(t+1) = w v_{id}(t) + c_1 r_1 (p_{best,id} - x_{id}(t)) + c_2 r_2 (g_{best,d} - x_{id}(t))$
- [ ] Position: $x_{id}(t+1) = x_{id}(t) + v_{id}(t+1)$
- [ ] Constriction: $\chi = \frac{2}{\vert 2 - \phi - \sqrt{\phi(\phi-4)} \vert}$
- [ ] Canonical: $\chi \approx 0.7298$, $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$ (or $c_1 = c_2 = 1.496$ with $w = 0.7298$)
- [ ] LDIW: $w(t) = w_{max} - \frac{(w_{max} - w_{min}) t}{T_{max}}$

**Key Parameters & Defaults:**
- [ ] $w \in [0.4, 0.9]$ (commonly starts at 0.9, decreases to 0.4)
- [ ] $c_1, c_2 \in [0, 2.5]$ (often both set to 2.05 or 1.496)
- [ ] Swarm size $N \in [20, 50]$ (sometimes up to 200)
- [ ] $V_{max} = k \cdot (x_{max} - x_{min})$ with $k \in [0.1, 1.0]$

**Algorithmic Steps (in order):**
1. Initialise position and velocity of all particles.
2. Evaluate fitness.
3. Initialise $p_{best}$ and $g_{best}$.
4. **Loop** until convergence:
   - Update velocity (clamp to $V_{max}$).
   - Update position (clamp to search space).
   - Evaluate new fitness.
   - Update $p_{best}$ if improved.
   - Update $g_{best}$ from all $p_{best}$ values.

**Topologies:**
- [ ] **Gbest (Star):** All particles informed by the single best — fast, low diversity.
- [ ] **Lbest (Ring):** Each particle informed by $k$ neighbours — slow, high diversity.
- [ ] **Von Neumann:** Grid-based — balanced.

**Dynamic Optimisation Extensions:**
- [ ] **Sentinel particle** for change detection.
- [ ] **Re-evaluation of all particles** on change.
- [ ] **Re-initialisation of worst particles** to restore diversity.
- [ ] **Memory archive** of past optima.
- [ ] **Multi-swarm** (convergent + divergent sub-swarms).
- [ ] **Charged PSO** with Coulomb-like repulsion.
- [ ] **Quantum PSO** with delta-potential well.

**Advantages of PSO:**
- [ ] Few parameters to tune.
- [ ] Continuous-variable native.
- [ ] Easy parallelisation (particles are independent).
- [ ] Fast convergence.
- [ ] Memory of past best (unlike GA's generational discard).

**Disadvantages of PSO:**
- [ ] Premature convergence in multimodal problems.
- [ ] No rigorous guarantee of finding the global optimum.
- [ ] Performance sensitive to $w$, $c_1$, $c_2$ tuning.
- [ ] Discrete problems require binary PSO variant.

**Applications to Mention in KTU Answers:**
- [ ] Neural network training (replacing back-propagation).
- [ ] Economic load dispatch in power systems.
- [ ] Antenna array optimisation.
- [ ] Robot path planning.
- [ ] Image segmentation.
- [ ] Function optimisation benchmarks (Rastrigin, Rosenbrock, Sphere, Ackley).

**Time & Space Complexity (cite if asked):**
- [ ] Time per iteration: $O(N \cdot D)$ for evaluation + $O(N \cdot D)$ for updates = $O(ND)$.
- [ ] Total time: $O(T_{max} \cdot N \cdot D)$.
- [ ] Space: $O(N \cdot D)$ to store positions, velocities, $p_{best}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Formula Card for the Exam Hall:**
> Carry a mental image of the **three-vector velocity update** — *inertia + cognitive + social* — and remember that the **position update is simply the velocity added to the position**. Everything else (clamping, $p_{best}$, $g_{best}$, constriction) is an enhancement on this core duality.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) Optimization, Biologically Inspired Algorithms in Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT862 - BIO INSPIRED OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) Optimization, Biologically Inspired Algorithms in Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 3 — ARTIFICIAL BEE COLONY (ABC) OPTIMIZATION

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) Algorithm** is a population-based, swarm-intelligence metaheuristic optimization technique proposed by **Derviş Karaboğa (2005)**. It is biologically inspired by the intelligent foraging behavior of a honey bee colony, where artificial "bees" (agents) collaboratively search for the optimal solution (food source with maximum nectar) in a multi-dimensional search space.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology:
- **ABC** belongs to the family of **Nature-Inspired Metaheuristic Algorithms (NIMA)**.
- It solves both **unconstrained** and **constrained** continuous and discrete optimization problems.
- It is classified as a **Swarm Intelligence (SI)** algorithm under the broader umbrella of **Computational Intelligence (CI)**.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — "The Honey Pot Problem"

Imagine **50 villagers** (bees) sent into a **vast forest** to find the spot with the **sweetest, most abundant fruit** (best solution). They cannot all search at once. So they divide roles:

| Real Bee Role | ABC Counterpart | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Employed Bee** | Active forager | Exploits a known food source, reports nectar amount |
| **Onlooker Bee** | Watcher at the hive | Picks the best-reported source probabilistically |
| **Scout Bee** | Explorer | Abandons a depleted source, randomly searches a new area |

> [!NOTE]
> **Nectar amount** in nature = **Objective function value (fitness)** in ABC.
> **Food source position** in nature = **Candidate solution vector (x₁, x₂, …, x_D)** in ABC.

### 1.2 The Three Phases of ABC

The ABC algorithm operates in **three sequential phases per iteration cycle**:

1. **Employed Bee Phase** → Exploitation of known sources
2. **Onlooker Bee Phase** → Probabilistic selection and further exploitation
3. **Scout Bee Phase** → Exploration through abandonment of stagnant sources

### 1.3 Why Biologically Inspired Algorithms in Engineering?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biologically Inspired (Bio-Inspired) Algorithms** are computational methods that mimic natural biological processes — evolution, swarm behavior, immune response, or neural activity — to solve complex engineering problems where classical methods (calculus-based, enumerative) fail.

**Key Reasons for Adoption in Engineering:**

- **Gradient-Free Operation**: Most real engineering problems (e.g., structural design, antenna tuning) have **no closed-form derivative**.
- **Global Search Capability**: Avoids getting trapped in local optima (a common KTU exam point).
- **Parallelism**: Population-based → naturally parallelizable on GPUs/distributed systems.
- **Robustness**: Tolerates noisy, dynamic, or discontinuous objective functions.
- **Black-Box Compatibility**: Only needs inputs and outputs, no internal model.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: Do NOT confuse **Bio-Inspired Algorithms** with **Bio-Informatics**. The former *use* biology to solve engineering problems; the latter *analyzes* biological data using algorithms.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Biological Foundation of ABC

A real honey bee colony exhibits **waggle dance communication**, where an employed bee that finds a rich food source returns to the hive and performs a dance. The **duration and intensity** of the dance is proportional to the **nectar quality**. Other bees (onlookers) then decide whether to follow that direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Waggle Dance Duration** ∝ **Food Source Profitability** — this is the **biological basis of the ABC probability selection rule**.

## 2.2 Mathematical Model of ABC

### 2.2.1 Problem Formulation

Given an optimization problem:

$$\min_{x \in \mathbb{R}^D} \; f(x) \quad \text{subject to} \quad lb_i \le x_i \le ub_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, D$$

where:
- $f(x)$ = objective (cost/loss) function
- $D$ = dimensionality of the problem
- $lb_i, ub_i$ = lower and upper bounds of the $i$-th decision variable
- $x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_D)$ = solution vector (food source position)

### 2.2.2 Colony Composition

Let the total colony size = $N_p$ (number of food sources).

- **Number of Employed Bees** = $N_p$ (one per food source)
- **Number of Onlooker Bees** = $N_p$
- **Number of Scout Bees** = Variable (assigned dynamically)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Convention**: Colony size = **2 × SN**, where **SN = Number of Food Sources** (typically 50% of colony).

### 2.2.3 Initial Population Generation

Food sources are initialized **uniformly at random** within the feasible bounds:

$$x_{i,j} = lb_j + \text{rand}(0, 1) \cdot (ub_j - lb_j)$$

where:
- $i = 1, 2, \ldots, SN$ (food source index)
- $j = 1, 2, \ldots, D$ (dimension index)
- $\text{rand}(0,1)$ = uniform random number in $[0, 1]$

## 2.3 Core Update Equation — The Heart of ABC

In the **employed bee phase**, a new candidate solution is generated by perturbing an existing food source:

$$v_{i,j} = x_{i,j} + \phi_{i,j} \cdot (x_{i,j} - x_{k,j})$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most important equation in ABC** — it is the **mutation/exploitation operator**.

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $v_{i,j}$ | New candidate solution (j-th component) |
| $x_{i,j}$ | Current solution of i-th bee |
| $x_{k,j}$ | Randomly chosen neighbor's solution ($k \ne i$) |
| $\phi_{i,j}$ | Uniform random number in $[-1, 1]$ |
| $j$ | Randomly chosen dimension index |

**Only ONE dimension $j$ is modified per candidate generation** — this is a deliberate design choice for computational efficiency.

## 2.4 Greedy Selection Mechanism

After generating $v_i$, ABC applies a **greedy selection**:

$$x_i^{\text{new}} = \begin{cases} v_i & \text{if } f(v_i) \le f(x_i) \quad \text{(minimization)} \\ x_i & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

## 2.5 Fitness Function Conversion

ABC requires **non-negative fitness values** (since it uses probability). For a minimization problem, fitness is:

$$\text{fit}_i = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{1 + f_i} & \text{if } f_i \ge 0 \\ 1 + \vert f_i \vert & \text{if } f_i < 0 \end{cases}$$

For maximization:

$$\text{fit}_i = f_i$$

## 2.6 Onlooker Phase — Probability Selection (Roulette Wheel)

Each onlooker selects a food source with probability:

$$p_i = \frac{\text{fit}_i}{\sum_{m=1}^{SN} \text{fit}_m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Higher fitness → Higher probability of selection.** This is a **roulette-wheel (fitness-proportional) selection** analogous to Genetic Algorithms.

Once selected, the onlooker performs the **same perturbation** as in the employed bee phase.

## 2.7 Scout Phase — Abandonment Rule

If a food source has **not improved** for a predefined number of iterations, called the **"Limit"** parameter, it is **abandoned** and replaced by a new random source:

$$\text{if trial}_i > \text{Limit} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Reinitialize } x_i$$

with a fresh random initialization using the population init formula.

**Trial counter** increments by 1 each time a source is not selected in the greedy step; resets to 0 upon improvement.

## 2.8 📊 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers ~70% of numerical/long-answer ABC questions in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Purpose / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initial Population | $x_{i,j} = lb_j + r \cdot (ub_j - lb_j)$ | Uniform seeding of candidate designs |
| 2 | New Solution (Mutation) | $v_{i,j} = x_{i,j} + \phi_{i,j}(x_{i,j} - x_{k,j})$ | Local search / exploitation |
| 3 | Greedy Selection | $v_i$ if better, else $x_i$ | Ensures monotonic improvement |
| 4 | Fitness (Min) | $\text{fit}_i = 1/(1+f_i)$ | Converts cost to probability-friendly value |
| 5 | Selection Probability | $p_i = \text{fit}_i / \sum \text{fit}_m$ | Roulette-wheel allocation of onlookers |
| 6 | Abandonment | if trial $\gt$ Limit → reinit | Diversification / exploration |
| 7 | Control Parameters | Colony Size, Limit, Max Cycle | Tuning knobs for convergence |
| 8 | Convergence | $\vert f^{\text{best}}_{\text{new}} - f^{\text{best}}_{\text{old}} \vert \le \epsilon$ | Termination criterion |

where $r \sim U(0,1)$ and $\phi \sim U(-1,1)$.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of ABC |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical Design** | Optimal shape of trusses, gear trains, heat exchangers |
| **Electrical/Electronics** | Optimal PID tuning, antenna array design, filter design |
| **Civil Engineering** | Structural optimization, water distribution networks |
| **Image Processing** | Image segmentation thresholding, feature selection |
| **Data Mining** | Neural network training, clustering (e.g., ABC + k-means) |
| **Wireless Networks** | Routing, node localization, energy efficiency |
| **Manufacturing** | Process parameter optimization in CNC, casting, welding |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the New Food Source Position

The ABC mutation equation is **not derived from first principles** in the classical sense — it is an **empirically designed** neighborhood search operator inspired by the local "neighborhood" concept in honey bee foraging. However, we can interpret it analytically.

**Step 1 — Identify Components**

Given the current food source $x_i$ and a randomly chosen partner $x_k$ ($k \ne i$), the search neighborhood is defined as the line segment joining $x_i$ and $x_k$ in the $D$-dimensional space.

**Step 2 — Define the Perturbation**

The perturbation vector is:

$$\Delta_{i,j} = \phi_{i,j} \cdot (x_{i,j} - x_{k,j})$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Perturbation**

$$v_{i,j} = x_{i,j} + \Delta_{i,j} = x_{i,j} + \phi_{i,j} \cdot (x_{i,j} - x_{k,j})$$

$$v_{i,j} = x_{i,j}(1 + \phi_{i,j}) - \phi_{i,j} \cdot x_{k,j}$$

**Step 4 — Bound Interpretation**

When $\phi_{i,j} = 0$, no change occurs. When $\phi_{i,j} = 1$, the new position is:

$$v_{i,j} = 2 x_{i,j} - x_{k,j}$$

This is the **mirror reflection** of $x_i$ across $x_k$, a common pattern in differential evolution.

**Step 5 — Expected Value**

Since $\phi_{i,j} \sim U(-1,1)$, $E[\phi_{i,j}] = 0$, hence:

$$E[v_{i,j}] = E[x_{i,j} + \phi_{i,j}(x_{i,j} - x_{k,j})] = x_{i,j} + 0 \cdot (x_{i,j} - x_{k,j}) = x_{i,j}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: The expected value of the new position equals the current position → **ABC performs an UNBIASED random walk** around $x_i$, which is statistically ideal for unbiased exploration.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Onlooker Probability Selection

**Step 1 — Total Fitness of the Colony**

$$\text{Fit}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{m=1}^{SN} \text{fit}_m$$

**Step 2 — Normalization Requirement**

For a valid probability, we require:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{SN} p_i = 1$$

**Step 3 — Apply Normalization**

$$p_i = \frac{\text{fit}_i}{\text{Fit}_{\text{total}}}$$

**Step 4 — Verification**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{SN} p_i = \sum_{i=1}^{SN} \frac{\text{fit}_i}{\sum_{m=1}^{SN} \text{fit}_m} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{SN} \text{fit}_i}{\sum_{m=1}^{SN} \text{fit}_m} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Monotonicity**

If $\text{fit}_i > \text{fit}_j$, then $p_i > p_j$ → **fitter sources get more onlooker visits** (exploitation pressure).

---

## 3.3 🎯 FULL WORKED EXAMPLE (KTU Numerical Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the type of question KTU asks in ESE — practice it thoroughly.**

**Problem**: Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 3)^2 + (x_2 - 2)^2$ using 2 iterations of ABC with colony size = 4 (SN=2), bounds $x_i \in [0, 5]$.

### **Iteration 0 — Initialization**

Random initial food sources (assume generated):

$$X_1 = (1.0, \; 4.0), \quad X_2 = (4.5, \; 0.5)$$

**Evaluate fitness** (using $f$ directly since $f \ge 0$):

$$f(X_1) = (1-3)^2 + (4-2)^2 = 4 + 4 = 8$$

$$f(X_2) = (4.5-3)^2 + (0.5-2)^2 = 2.25 + 2.25 = 4.5$$

**Convert to fitness** ($\text{fit} = 1/(1+f)$):

$$\text{fit}_1 = \frac{1}{1+8} = \frac{1}{9} \approx 0.1111$$

$$\text{fit}_2 = \frac{1}{1+4.5} = \frac{1}{5.5} \approx 0.1818$$

### **Iteration 1 — Employed Bee Phase**

**For Bee 1**: Pick random neighbor $k = 2$, random dimension $j = 1$, $\phi = 0.6$.

$$v_{1,1} = x_{1,1} + \phi \cdot (x_{1,1} - x_{2,1}) = 1.0 + 0.6 \cdot (1.0 - 4.5) = 1.0 + 0.6 \cdot (-3.5)$$

$$v_{1,1} = 1.0 - 2.1 = -1.1$$

Since $-1.1 \notin [0, 5]$, **clip** to $v_1 = (0, 4.0)$ (boundary handling).

**Greedy check**: $f(0, 4) = 9 + 4 = 13 > 8$ → **Reject**, keep $X_1$.

**For Bee 2**: Pick neighbor $k = 1$, $j = 2$, $\phi = -0.4$.

$$v_{2,2} = x_{2,2} + \phi \cdot (x_{2,2} - x_{1,2}) = 0.5 + (-0.4) \cdot (0.5 - 4.0)$$

$$v_{2,2} = 0.5 + (-0.4)(-3.5) = 0.5 + 1.4 = 1.9$$

**Greedy check**: $f(4.5, 1.9) = 2.25 + 0.01 = 2.26 < 4.5$ → **Accept** $V_2 = (4.5, 1.9)$.

### **Onlooker Phase**

Probabilities:

$$p_1 = \frac{0.1111}{0.1111 + 0.1818} = \frac{0.1111}{0.2929} \approx 0.3793$$

$$p_2 = \frac{0.1818}{0.2929} \approx 0.6207$$

With 2 onlookers and a random number $r = 0.55$:

- Onlooker 1: $r > p_1 = 0.379$ → chooses **Source 2**
- Onlooker 2: $r > p_2$ since $0.55 < 0.6207$ (cumulative) → chooses **Source 2**

Both onlookers exploit Source 2. (In practice, multiple onlookers may visit the best source.)

### **Iteration 2 — Continued refinement**

The algorithm proceeds; if a source's trial counter exceeds **Limit = 100**, it is reinitialized.

### **Final Answer**

After convergence, the algorithm should converge near the optimum:

$$x^* = (3, 2), \quad f(x^*) = 0$$

---

## 3.4 Comparative Algorithmic Position

| Algorithm | Inspiration | Year | Update Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| GA (Genetic Algorithm) | Darwinian Evolution | 1975 | Crossover + Mutation |
| PSO (Particle Swarm) | Bird flocking | 1995 | Velocity-position update |
| **ABC (Artificial Bee Colony)** | **Bee foraging** | **2005** | **Neighborhood perturbation** |
| ACO (Ant Colony) | Ant pheromone trails | 1991 | Pheromone deposition |
| Firefly Algorithm | Firefly luminescence | 2008 | Attraction-based movement |
| Cuckoo Search | Cuckoo parasitism | 2009 | Lévy flight + random walk |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favorite Comparison**: ABC vs PSO — both are swarm-based, but ABC uses **discrete role-based agents** (employed/onlooker/scout), while PSO uses a **single uniform behavior** (velocity update with inertia + cognitive + social terms).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 ABC Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize Parameters] --> B[Generate Initial Food Sources SN]
    B --> C[Evaluate Fitness fit i for all sources]
    C --> D[Set trial counter trial i = 0]
    D --> E[Iteration cycle = 1 to MaxCycle]
    E --> F[Employed Bee Phase]
    F --> G[Generate new solution v i using neighbor x k]
    G --> H[Apply greedy selection]
    H --> I{Is v i better than x i?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Accept v i and reset trial i = 0]
    I -->|No| K[Reject and increment trial i = trial i + 1]
    J --> L[Onlooker Bee Phase]
    K --> L[Onlooker Bee Phase]
    L --> M[Compute probability p i for each source]
    M --> N[Onlooker selects source via roulette wheel]
    N --> O[Generate new candidate and apply greedy selection]
    O --> P[Scout Bee Phase]
    P --> Q{Any trial i greater than Limit?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Abandon source and reinitialize randomly]
    Q -->|No| S[Keep source unchanged]
    R --> T[Update best solution found so far]
    S --> T[Update best solution found so far]
    T --> U{cycle equals MaxCycle or converged?}
    U -->|No| E
    U -->|Yes| V[Output best solution x best and f best]
    V --> W[Stop]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of ABC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["INPUT LAYER"]
        A1[Problem Definition f x]
        A2[Bounds lb and ub]
        A3[ABC Parameters SN Limit MaxCycle]
    end

        B1[Population Initializer]
        B2[Fitness Evaluator]

        B6[Best Solution Tracker]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        C1[Optimal Solution x star]
        C2[Optimal Fitness f star]
        C3[Convergence Curve]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5

    B6 --> B3
    B6 --> C1
    B6 --> C2
    B6 --> C3
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**
**Define the Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) algorithm. List the three types of artificial bees and state the role of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **ABC Algorithm**: It is a swarm-intelligence-based metaheuristic optimization algorithm proposed by Derviş Karaboğa (2005), inspired by the foraging behavior of honey bees. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Three types of bees and their roles: (2 marks)**
> 1. **Employed Bees** — Associated with a specific food source; perform local search by perturbing the current position and report nectar amount to onlookers via "waggle dance."
> 2. **Onlooker Bees** — Stay in the hive; probabilistically select food sources based on nectar information shared by employed bees, then perform local exploitation.
> 3. **Scout Bees** — When a food source is exhausted (trial counter exceeds the Limit), the employed bee becomes a scout and randomly searches for a new food source.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Write the expression for generating a new candidate solution in the ABC algorithm. What is the range of the random parameter used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **New candidate solution equation** in the employed bee phase: **(2 marks)**
>
> $$v_{i,j} = x_{i,j} + \phi_{i,j} \cdot (x_{i,j} - x_{k,j})$$
>
> where $x_i$ is the current source, $x_k$ is a randomly chosen neighbor, $j$ is a random dimension, and $\phi_{i,j} \in [-1, +1]$ is a uniform random number. **(1 mark)**
>
> Only one dimension $j$ is updated per candidate to balance exploration and exploitation.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full-Length KTU ESE Pattern**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model QP, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the biological inspiration behind the Artificial Bee Colony algorithm. Discuss the waggle dance mechanism and its mathematical analogy in ABC. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the complete step-by-step procedure of the ABC algorithm. Explain the role of the **Limit** parameter in the scout bee phase. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Biological Inspiration and Waggle Dance Analogy (7 marks)**

Honey bees exhibit **collective intelligence** to locate and exploit rich food sources efficiently. When a bee discovers a profitable nectar source, it returns to the hive and performs a **waggle dance** on the comb.

- The **angle** of the dance indicates the **direction** of the food source relative to the sun.
- The **duration** of the dance is **proportional to the nectar quality (profitability)**.

In ABC, this is mathematically mapped as follows:

| Biological Concept | ABC Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Nectar amount | Objective function value $f(x_i)$ |
| Waggle dance duration | Fitness $\text{fit}_i$ |
| Following another bee's direction | Onlooker selecting source via probability $p_i$ |
| Depletion of a source | Trial counter exceeding Limit |

The selection probability given by $p_i = \text{fit}_i / \sum \text{fit}_m$ **mathematically mimics** the dance-duration-based recruitment in real bees — better sources "dance longer" and attract more followers. **(7 marks — full marks for: inspiration explanation 2 marks, waggle dance description 2 marks, mathematical mapping 3 marks)**

---

**(b) Step-by-Step ABC Procedure and Limit Parameter (7 marks)**

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Initialization**: Define colony size $2 \times SN$, Limit, MaxCycle. Generate $SN$ food sources uniformly in $[lb, ub]$. Evaluate fitness. Initialize trial counters to 0. **(1 mark)**

2. **Employed Bee Phase**: Each employed bee generates a new candidate $v_i$ using $v_{i,j} = x_{i,j} + \phi(x_{i,j} - x_{k,j})$. Apply greedy selection; if better, replace and reset trial; else increment trial. **(1 mark)**

3. **Onlooker Bee Phase**: Compute probability $p_i = \text{fit}_i / \sum \text{fit}_m$. Each onlooker selects a source via roulette wheel and performs local search. Apply greedy selection. **(1 mark)**

4. **Scout Bee Phase**: Identify the source with the **maximum trial counter**. If trial $\gt$ Limit, **abandon** and reinitialize that source randomly. **(1 mark)**

5. **Update Best**: Track the best solution found so far. **(1 mark)**

6. **Termination**: Stop when cycle = MaxCycle or $\vert f_{\text{new}} - f_{\text{old}} \vert \le \epsilon$. **(1 mark)**

**Role of the Limit Parameter:**

The **Limit** is a critical control parameter that prevents the algorithm from getting stuck in local optima. It represents the **maximum number of cycles a food source can remain unimproved** before being abandoned. **(1 mark)**

- **Small Limit** → More frequent abandonment → Higher exploration, slower convergence.
- **Large Limit** → Less abandonment → Higher exploitation, risk of premature convergence.

> A commonly recommended value is **Limit = SN × D**, where SN is the number of food sources and D is the dimensionality.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model QP, Module 3]**

**(a)** Differentiate between the **Employed Bee Phase** and the **Onlooker Bee Phase** of ABC. How does the probability selection mechanism work in the onlooker phase? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A minimization problem has the following food sources in iteration $t$:
$X_1 = (2, 1)$ with $f(X_1) = 5$
$X_2 = (1, 3)$ with $f(X_2) = 10$

For employed bee 1, using neighbor $k = 2$, dimension $j = 2$, and $\phi = 0.5$, generate the new candidate. Apply greedy selection. Then compute the probabilities for the onlooker phase. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Employed vs Onlooker Bee Phase (7 marks)**

| Aspect | Employed Bee Phase | Onlooker Bee Phase |
|---|---|---|
| **Number** | Equal to SN | Equal to SN |
| **Source** | Pre-assigned to a source | Selected probabilistically |
| **Action** | Exploits its known source | Exploits the selected source |
| **Selection** | Deterministic (one per source) | Probabilistic (roulette wheel) |
| **Information** | Reports fitness to onlookers | Receives fitness information |
| **Role** | Information provider | Information consumer |

**(3 marks for the comparison table; 1 mark for conclusion on role division)**

**Probability Selection Mechanism (3 marks):**

The fitness is first computed (using $\text{fit}_i = 1/(1+f_i)$ for non-negative minimization). Then:

$$p_i = \frac{\text{fit}_i}{\sum_{m=1}^{SN} \text{fit}_m}$$

A cumulative probability distribution is built, and a uniform random number $r \in [0,1]$ is generated. The first $i$ for which $r \le \sum_{j=1}^{i} p_j$ is selected. This ensures **fitter sources are selected more often**, mimicking waggle dance recruitment.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Fitness conversion** (1 mark):

$$\text{fit}_1 = \frac{1}{1+5} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667$$

$$\text{fit}_2 = \frac{1}{1+10} = \frac{1}{11} \approx 0.0909$$

**Step 2 — Generate new candidate for Bee 1** (3 marks):

Using $j = 2$, $\phi = 0.5$, $k = 2$:

$$v_{1,2} = x_{1,2} + \phi \cdot (x_{1,2} - x_{2,2})$$

$$v_{1,2} = 1 + 0.5 \cdot (1 - 3) = 1 + 0.5 \cdot (-2) = 1 - 1 = 0$$

So $V_1 = (2, 0)$.

**Step 3 — Greedy selection** (1 mark):

$$f(2, 0) = (2-3)^2 + (0-2)^2 = 1 + 4 = 5$$

Wait — actual $f$ here is unspecified, but assuming $f(2,0) = 5$ for illustration. Since $f(V_1) \le f(X_1)$ (equal or better), **accept** $V_1$.

If $f(V_1) > f(X_1)$, reject and keep $X_1$, increment trial counter.

**Step 4 — Compute onlooker probabilities** (2 marks):

$$p_1 = \frac{0.1667}{0.1667 + 0.0909} = \frac{0.1667}{0.2576} \approx 0.6471$$

$$p_2 = \frac{0.0909}{0.2576} \approx 0.3529$$

**Verification**: $0.6471 + 0.3529 = 1.0000$ ✓

> Since $p_1 > p_2$, **Source 1 is more likely to be selected** by onlookers because it has a higher fitness (lower objective value).

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that COST MARKS in KTU ESE:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the fitness conversion** for minimization problems — many students directly use $f(x)$ as probability, causing negative values and selection errors. **Always convert to $\text{fit}_i = 1/(1+f_i)$ first.**
>
> 2. **Confusing $x_{i,j}$ and $x_{k,j}$** in the mutation equation — $k$ is a **randomly chosen partner**, NOT a specific neighbor index. KTU examiners often test this.
>
> 3. **Writing $\phi \in [0,1]$ instead of $[-1,1]$** — the standard ABC uses **symmetric** uniform distribution. This is a 1-mark loss.
>
> 4. **Skipping the greedy selection** step — without it, ABC loses its convergence guarantee.
>
> 5. **Calling ABC a Genetic Algorithm** — they are different. GA uses crossover and mutation on chromosomes; ABC uses neighborhood perturbation on continuous positions.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to update the trial counter** — the scout phase logic fails if trial counters are not maintained.
>
> 7. **Missing the "Limit" parameter justification** — it is the **key diversification mechanism** of ABC; examiners expect an explanation.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 5-minute revision before KTU ESE — go through this list mentally:**

- ✅ ABC = Bio-inspired swarm intelligence algorithm (Karaboğa, 2005) for global optimization.
- ✅ Three bee types: **Employed** (exploit known), **Onlooker** (select by probability), **Scout** (reinit stagnant).
- ✅ **Colony size** = 2 × SN; standard SN = 50.
- ✅ **Mutation equation**: $v_{i,j} = x_{i,j} + \phi_{i,j}(x_{i,j} - x_{k,j})$; only ONE dimension changed.
- ✅ $\phi \in [-1, 1]$; $k \ne i$; $j$ is random.
- ✅ **Greedy selection** ensures monotonic improvement.
- ✅ **Fitness**: $\text{fit}_i = 1/(1+f_i)$ for minimization.
- ✅ **Probability**: $p_i = \text{fit}_i / \sum \text{fit}_m$ (roulette-wheel).
- ✅ **Limit parameter** = SN × D (standard); triggers scout abandonment.
- ✅ **Termination**: MaxCycle or convergence ($\vert \Delta f \vert \le \epsilon$).
- ✅ ABC advantages: Gradient-free, parallelizable, robust, simple.
- ✅ ABC disadvantages: Slow convergence near optimum (no acceleration term like PSO's velocity).
- ✅ Bio-inspired algorithms in engineering: PSO, GA, ABC, ACO, Firefly, Cuckoo, Bat, Grey Wolf.
- ✅ ABC applications: Truss design, PID tuning, image segmentation, neural net training, antenna design.
- ✅ ABC vs PSO: ABC has **discrete roles**; PSO has **continuous velocity update** with inertia weight.
- ✅ Key bio-mapping: **Waggle dance ↔ Fitness probability**; **Foraging ↔ Optimization search**; **Exhaustion ↔ Trial > Limit**.

> **🎯 End of Module 3 Notes — Best wishes for your KTU ESE preparation!**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Introduction to Advanced Swarm Intelligence

Swarm Intelligence (SI) refers to the collective, decentralized, self-organized behaviour emerging from simple agents interacting locally with each other and their environment. While the earlier modules extensively covered **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)**, **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)**, and **Bee Colony Algorithms**, this module extends the bio-inspired paradigm to three more elegant natural systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a high-weightage, theory-intensive section in Module 4. Questions frequently appear in **Part B (14 marks)** format, testing both conceptual understanding and mathematical modeling of swarm behaviours.

---

## 1.2 Artificial Fish Swarm Algorithm (AFSA)

### Formal Definition
The **Artificial Fish Swarm Algorithm (AFSA)** is a population-based meta-heuristic optimization technique proposed by **Li Xiao-Lei et al. in 2002**. It mimics the collective social behaviour of fish schooling in nature, where each artificial fish moves through a search space by executing three primary behaviours: **Preying, Swarming, and Following**, combined with **random movement** to explore and exploit the solution space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Term (KTU):** *Fish Swarm Algorithm* — formally referred to as *Artificial Fish Swarm Algorithm (AFSA)* in standard optimization literature.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a school of fish swimming in a large murky lake. Each fish wants to find the spot in the lake with the most food (the optimal solution). The fish cannot see the entire lake at once; it can only sense the food concentration in its immediate surroundings. However, by observing the behaviour of its nearby companions, each fish makes smart decisions:

- If one fish suddenly darts forward eagerly, others follow (that's where food might be).
- If fish are crowded in one spot but not too congested, they drift toward the centre of the group (safety in numbers).
- When food seems scarce, they randomly explore new waters.

This collective, rule-based behaviour is exactly what AFSA simulates mathematically.

### Key Behavioural Constants
- **Visual (V):** The maximum distance a fish can perceive.
- **Step (S):** The maximum distance a fish moves in one iteration.
- **δ (Crowd Factor):** A threshold controlling the degree of crowding allowed.
- **try_number:** The maximum number of prey attempts in one iteration.

---

## 1.3 Bacteria Foraging Optimization Algorithm (BFOA)

### Formal Definition
The **Bacteria Foraging Optimization Algorithm (BFOA)** is a biologically inspired meta-heuristic introduced by **Kevin M. Passino in 2001**. It models the foraging strategies of the *Escherichia coli* bacterium through four principal mechanisms: **Chemotaxis, Swarming, Reproduction, and Elimination-Dispersal**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Picture a colony of bacteria in a Petri dish containing nutrients. Each bacterium is a tiny blind organism that:
1. **Swims (chemotaxis)** — tumbles and runs toward regions with higher nutrient concentration.
2. **Communicates (swarming)** — releases attractant signals and repels at very close range, forming circular swarms.
3. **Reproduces (reproduction)** — healthier bacteria (those that found more nutrients) split into two, while weaker ones die off.
4. **Migrates (elimination-dispersal)** — occasionally, some bacteria are displaced to new locations by environmental events.

This is precisely the lifecycle simulated by BFOA to locate the global optimum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** *Chemotactic step size* **C(i)** is critical — too large causes oscillation, too small causes slow convergence.

---

## 1.4 Intelligent Water Drop (IWD) Algorithm

### Formal Definition
The **Intelligent Water Drop (IWD) Algorithm** was proposed by **Shah-Hosseini (2007)**. It is a nature-inspired optimization method based on the dynamics of water drops flowing in a river. The algorithm models how water drops modify the path soil (forming rivers) and how faster water drops gather more soil, thereby creating a positive feedback loop that finds optimal paths between two points.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine raindrops flowing down a mountainside. Initially, drops trickle everywhere. As drops flow, they erode the soil along their paths. Subsequent drops find the path with less soil and travel faster along it. Faster drops erode even more soil, deepening the channel, which attracts even more drops. Eventually, a well-defined riverbed forms — the most efficient path. The IWD algorithm mathematically simulates this soil-erosion dynamic to find optimal routes or solutions.

### Key Physical Constructs
- **Soil:** Carried by the water drop and removed from the path.
- **Velocity:** Inversely proportional to the soil in the path; higher velocity drops collect less soil.
- **Time:** Time taken to traverse a path, used in the soil update rule.

---

## 1.5 Applications of Biologically Inspired Algorithms

Biologically inspired algorithms (BIA) form a powerful class of computational tools that solve complex real-world engineering problems. KTU 2024 highlights their role in modern optimization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Application Domain Map:**
> - **Structural Engineering:** Truss and frame design optimization.
> - **Electrical Engineering:** Power system load dispatch, economic dispatch.
> - **Computer Science:** Feature selection, neural network training, image segmentation.
> - **Mechanical Engineering:** Job-shop scheduling, robot path planning, gearbox design.
> - **Civil Engineering:** Water distribution network design, traffic routing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Artificial Fish Swarm Algorithm (AFSA) — Deep Analysis

### 2.1.1 Operational Concept
AFSA operates on a population of `N` artificial fish, each represented by a state vector `X_i = (x_1, x_2, ..., x_n)`. The objective is to find `X^*` that minimizes (or maximizes) a fitness function `Y = f(X)`. The algorithm proceeds in **four fundamental behaviours**:

#### Behaviour 1: AF_Prey (Preying)
The fish attempts to move toward a randomly chosen position with better food concentration.

$$
X_{next} = X_i + \text{Rand}(\cdot) \cdot \text{Step} \cdot \frac{X_j - X_i}{\Vert X_j - X_i \Vert}
$$

Where `X_j` is a randomly selected point within the visual range such that `f(X_j) < f(X_i)`. If no better point is found after `try_number` attempts, random movement is executed.

#### Behaviour 2: AF_Swarm (Swarming)
The fish evaluates the centre of mass of its neighbours and moves toward it if it is not too crowded.

**Step 1:** Compute the centre position:

$$
X_c = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{n_f} X_j}{n_f}
$$

Where `n_f` is the number of fish within the visual range.

**Step 2:** Check crowding condition:

$$
\frac{n_f}{N} < \delta
$$

If true and `f(X_c) < f(X_i)`, the fish moves toward the centre:

$$
X_{next} = X_i + \text{Rand}(\cdot) \cdot \text{Step} \cdot \frac{X_c - X_i}{\Vert X_c - X_i \Vert}
$$

#### Behaviour 3: AF_Follow (Following)
The fish identifies the best neighbour in its visual range (with the lowest food concentration for minimization) and follows it if the position is not crowded.

**Step 1:** Find `X_max` — the neighbour with the best fitness.

**Step 2:** Apply crowding check `n_f / N < δ`.

**Step 3:** If valid, move toward `X_max`:

$$
X_{next} = X_i + \text{Rand}(\cdot) \cdot \text{Step} \cdot \frac{X_{max} - X_i}{\Vert X_{max} - X_i \Vert}
$$

#### Behaviour 4: AF_Move (Random Movement)
If none of the above behaviours yield a better state, the fish moves randomly within its visual range:

$$
X_{next} = X_i + \text{Rand}(\cdot) \cdot \text{Step}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Bulleted Logic of AFSA Decision Flow:**
> 1. For each fish, explore visual range.
> 2. Compute swarming and following candidates.
> 3. Execute the behaviour that yields the greatest fitness improvement.
> 4. Update fish position and bulletin global best.
> 5. Repeat until convergence.

---

## 2.2 Bacteria Foraging Optimization Algorithm (BFOA) — Deep Analysis

### 2.2.1 The Three-Tiered Lifecycle
BFOA formalizes the E. coli foraging lifecycle as three nested loops. Let:
- `p` = dimension of search space
- `S` = total number of bacteria
- `N_c` = number of chemotactic steps
- `N_s` = swim length
- `N_re` = number of reproduction steps
- `N_ed` = number of elimination-dispersal events
- `P_ed` = probability of elimination-dispersal

#### Phase 1: Chemotaxis
A bacterium moves by **tumbling** (random direction) or **swimming** (in the same direction). The position update is:

$$
\theta_i(j+1, k, l) = \theta_i(j, k, l) + C(i) \cdot \Delta(i)
$$

Where:
- `θ_i(j, k, l)` = position of *i-th* bacterium at *j-th* chemotactic step, *k-th* reproduction, *l-th* elimination.
- `C(i)` = chemotactic step size.
- `Δ(i)` = unit length random direction vector.

If the new position improves fitness, the bacterium continues swimming in the same direction for up to `N_s` steps.

#### Phase 2: Swarming
Bacteria release attractant and repellant signals. The cell-to-cell signalling function is:

$$
J_{cc}(\theta, P(j, k, l)) = \sum_{i=1}^{S} J_{cc}^{i}(\theta, \theta_i(j, k, l))
$$

Where the individual signalling effect is:

$$
J_{cc}^{i}(\theta, \theta_i) = -d_{attract} \exp\left(-w_{attract} \sum_{m=1}^{p}(\theta_m - \theta_m^i)^2\right) + h_{repellant} \exp\left(-w_{repellant} \sum_{m=1}^{p}(\theta_m - \theta_m^i)^2\right)
$$

This causes bacteria to swarm into rings.

#### Phase 3: Reproduction
The health of each bacterium is computed as the sum of its fitness over the chemotactic lifetime:

$$
J_{health}^i = \sum_{j=1}^{N_c+1} f(i, j, k, l)
$$

Bacteria are sorted by health. The `S/2` healthiest bacteria split into two each (asexual reproduction), and the `S/2` weakest die.

#### Phase 4: Elimination-Dispersal
With probability `P_ed`, each bacterium is reinitialized to a random position in the search space. This prevents premature convergence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** BFOA excels in problems with **non-gradient, noisy fitness landscapes** such as optimal controller tuning and harmonic estimation in power systems.

---

## 2.3 Intelligent Water Drop (IWD) Algorithm — Deep Analysis

### 2.3.1 The Soil-Based Dynamic System
The IWD algorithm constructs a path incrementally. Each IWD has two key properties: **velocity (vel)** and **soil (soil)**. The algorithm proceeds through three operators:

#### Operator 1: Path Construction
At each step, an IWD chooses the next node from unvisited nodes using a probability function inversely proportional to the soil on the connecting path:

$$
p_i^{IWD}(t) = \frac{f(soil(i, IWD))}{\sum_{j \notin vc(IWD)} f(soil(j, IWD))}
$$

Where `vc(IWD)` is the set of already-visited nodes, and `f(soil) = 1 / (ε_0 + g(soil))` with `g(soil) = soil` and `ε_0` a small positive constant.

#### Operator 2: Soil Update (Local)
When an IWD traverses a path from node `i` to node `j`, the soil on the path is reduced:

$$
\Delta soil(i, j) = \frac{A_v}{B_v + C_v \cdot time(i, j; vel(t))}
$$

Where `time(i, j; vel(t))` is given by the inverse velocity:

$$
time(i, j; vel(t)) = \frac{HU(i, j)}{vel(t)}
$$

The path soil is updated as:

$$
soil(i, j) = (1 - \rho_n) \cdot soil(i, j) - \rho_n \cdot \Delta soil(i, j)
$$

#### Operator 3: Velocity Update
The IWD's velocity is updated based on the inverse of the time taken:

$$
vel^{IWD}(t+1) = vel^{IWD}(t) + \frac{a_v}{b_v + c_v \cdot soil(i, j)}
$$

The IWD's own soil increases (it picks up soil from the path):

$$
soil^{IWD}(t+1) = soil^{IWD}(t) + \Delta soil(i, j)
$$

#### Operator 4: Global Soil Update
After all IWDs complete their paths, the best IWD reinforces its path:

$$
soil(i, j) = (1 - \rho_{IWD}) \cdot soil(i, j) - \rho_{IWD} \cdot \frac{1}{T^{IB}} \quad \text{for } (i, j) \in T^{IB}
$$

Where `T^{IB}` is the best tour's total soil and `ρ_IWD` is the learning rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** IWD is widely applied in **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)**, **vehicle routing**, **network routing**, and **multi-objective combinatorial optimization**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all critical formulas** required for the KTU 2024 examination. Memorize these — they appear in **Part B derivations** every cycle.

| **Algorithm** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Parameters** |
|---------------|-------------|-----------------|----------------|
| AFSA Prey | $X_{next} = X_i + \text{Rand} \cdot S \cdot \frac{X_j - X_i}{\Vert X_j - X_i \Vert}$ | Fish moves toward better food | Step `S`, Visual `V` |
| AFSA Swarm Centre | $X_c = \frac{\sum X_j}{n_f}$ | Centre of mass of neighbours | `n_f` neighbours |
| AFSA Crowding Check | $\frac{n_f}{N} < \delta$ | Avoid over-crowding | Crowd factor `δ` |
| BFOA Chemotaxis | $\theta_i(j+1) = \theta_i(j) + C(i) \cdot \Delta(i)$ | Tumble + Run | Step `C(i)`, vector `Δ` |
| BFOA Swarming | $J_{cc} = -d_{attract} e^{-w_{attract} d^2} + h_{rep} e^{-w_{rep} d^2}$ | Cell signalling | Attract/Repel coeff. |
| BFOA Health | $J_{health}^i = \sum_{j=1}^{N_c+1} f(i, j, k, l)$ | Reproduction fitness | Sum of chemo fitness |
| IWD Time | $time(i, j) = \frac{HU(i, j)}{vel}$ | Time on edge | Heuristic `HU` |
| IWD ΔSoil | $\Delta soil = \frac{A_v}{B_v + C_v \cdot time}$ | Soil removed from path | Constants `A, B, C` |
| IWD Velocity | $vel(t+1) = vel(t) + \frac{a_v}{b_v + c_v \cdot soil}$ | Drop accelerates | Constants `a, b, c` |
| IWD Probability | $p_i(t) = \frac{f(soil_i)}{\sum f(soil_j)}$ | Selection probability | Function `f` |
| AFSA Visual | $\Vert X_j - X_i \Vert \leq V$ | Neighbouring range | Visual `V` |
| BFOA Elim-Disp | $P = P_{ed}$ for each bacterium | Reinit probability | Probability `P_ed` |

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of AFSA Position Update (Prey Behaviour)

### Problem Context
We need to derive the canonical position update rule for an artificial fish executing the **prey behaviour**, given that the fish is at position `X_i` and a randomly sampled target point `X_j` exists within the visual range `V` with strictly better fitness.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the displacement vector from current position to the target:**

$$
\vec{d} = X_j - X_i
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Euclidean norm (magnitude) of the displacement:**

$$
\Vert \vec{d} \Vert = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{n}(x_{j,k} - x_{i,k})^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Construct the unit direction vector (normalization):**

$$
\hat{u} = \frac{\vec{d}}{\Vert \vec{d} \Vert} = \frac{X_j - X_i}{\Vert X_j - X_i \Vert}
$$

This unit vector points from `X_i` toward `X_j` with magnitude exactly 1.

**Step 4 — Apply the random step scaling:**

$$
\vec{s} = \text{Rand}(\cdot) \cdot S \cdot \hat{u}
$$

Where:
- `Rand(·)` ∈ [0, 1] ensures stochastic exploration.
- `S` is the maximum step size, constraining the move length.

**Step 5 — Compute the new position by vector addition:**

$$
X_{next} = X_i + \vec{s} = X_i + \text{Rand}(\cdot) \cdot S \cdot \frac{X_j - X_i}{\Vert X_j - X_i \Vert}
$$

### Final Boxed Result
$$
\boxed{X_{next} = X_i + \text{Rand}(\cdot) \cdot S \cdot \frac{X_j - X_i}{\Vert X_j - X_i \Vert}}
$$

### Engineering Interpretation
The normalization step ensures that the fish moves a **consistent step size** `S` regardless of the absolute distance to the target. Without normalization, a distant target would cause huge jumps, while a close target would yield tiny steps — destroying search stability.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of BFOA Cell-to-Cell Swarming Function

### Problem Context
We need to derive the cell-to-cell signalling function `J_cc(θ, P)` that drives swarming behaviour in BFOA. The signalling effect consists of two parts: an **attractant** component (pulls bacteria together at moderate distance) and a **repellant** component (pushes them apart at very close range).

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the squared Euclidean distance between bacterium positions:**

$$
d_i^2 = \sum_{m=1}^{p}(\theta_m - \theta_m^i)^2
$$

Where `p` is the search-space dimension and `θ_m^i` is the *m-th* coordinate of the *i-th* bacterium.

**Step 2 — Define the attractant intensity profile:**

Bacteria release attractant signal. The intensity at a remote point `θ` due to a bacterium at `θ^i` decays exponentially with distance:

$$
J_{attract}^{i}(\theta) = -d_{attract} \cdot \exp(-w_{attract} \cdot d_i^2)
$$

The negative sign indicates the signal is attractive (pulls the search toward better regions in minimization problems).

**Step 3 — Define the repellant intensity profile:**

Bacteria also release repellant. The intensity profile is:

$$
J_{repellant}^{i}(\theta) = +h_{repellant} \cdot \exp(-w_{repellant} \cdot d_i^2)
$$

The positive sign indicates a repulsive effect that prevents overcrowding.

**Step 4 — Combine the two effects into a single signalling function:**

$$
J_{cc}^{i}(\theta, \theta^i) = -d_{attract} \exp(-w_{attract} \cdot d_i^2) + h_{repellant} \exp(-w_{repellant} \cdot d_i^2)
$$

**Step 5 — Sum over all bacteria in the population:**

$$
J_{cc}(\theta, P) = \sum_{i=1}^{S} J_{cc}^{i}(\theta, \theta^i)
$$

$$
J_{cc}(\theta, P) = \sum_{i=1}^{S} \left[ -d_{attract} \exp(-w_{attract} \cdot d_i^2) + h_{repellant} \exp(-w_{repellant} \cdot d_i^2) \right]
$$

**Step 6 — Add the dynamic fitness function (final cost):**

The bacterium's effective objective combines the underlying problem `J(i, j, k, l)` with the signalling function:

$$
J(i, j, k, l) = J_{problem}(\theta_i) + J_{cc}(\theta_i, P(j, k, l))
$$

### Final Boxed Result
$$
\boxed{J_{cc}(\theta, P) = \sum_{i=1}^{S} \left[ -d_{attract} e^{-w_{attract} d_i^2} + h_{repellant} e^{-w_{repellant} d_i^2} \right]}
$$

### Engineering Interpretation
The dual-signal mechanism (attract + repel) creates a **self-organizing ring-shaped swarm pattern** that allows the algorithm to:
1. Gather around good solutions (attract).
2. Avoid degenerate convergence to a single point (repel).
3. Maintain population diversity throughout the search.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of IWD Soil and Velocity Coupled Update

### Problem Context
We need to derive the **velocity update** and **soil update** equations that govern an intelligent water drop's movement between nodes `i` and `j`.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the initial velocity and soil of the IWD:**

At time `t = 0`:
- `vel(0) = vel_init` (a small positive initial velocity).
- `soil(0) = soil_init` (a small positive initial soil).

**Step 2 — Define the time to traverse edge (i, j):**

By kinematics, time = distance / velocity. The "distance" is the heuristic desirability `HU(i, j)`:

$$
time(i, j; vel(t)) = \frac{HU(i, j)}{vel(t)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the soil removed from the path:**

The amount of soil removed from the path is inversely proportional to traversal time (faster drops remove less):

$$
\Delta soil(i, j) = \frac{A_v}{B_v + C_v \cdot time(i, j; vel(t))}
$$

Substituting the time:

$$
\Delta soil(i, j) = \frac{A_v}{B_v + C_v \cdot \frac{HU(i, j)}{vel(t)}} = \frac{A_v \cdot vel(t)}{B_v \cdot vel(t) + C_v \cdot HU(i, j)}
$$

**Step 4 — Update the path soil:**

$$
soil(i, j) \leftarrow (1 - \rho_n) \cdot soil(i, j) - \rho_n \cdot \Delta soil(i, j)
$$

Where `ρ_n` ∈ (0, 1) is the local soil update rate.

**Step 5 — Update the IWD's velocity:**

The drop accelerates proportional to soil removed (Newtonian-inspired feedback):

$$
vel(t+1) = vel(t) + \frac{a_v}{b_v + c_v \cdot soil(i, j)}
$$

**Step 6 — Update the IWD's own soil (it picks up what was removed):**

$$
soil^{IWD}(t+1) = soil^{IWD}(t) + \Delta soil(i, j)
$$

### Final Boxed Result

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\Delta soil(i, j) &= \frac{A_v}{B_v + C_v \cdot \frac{HU(i, j)}{vel(t)}} \\
vel(t+1) &= vel(t) + \frac{a_v}{b_v + c_v \cdot soil(i, j)} \\
soil(i, j) &\leftarrow (1 - \rho_n) \cdot soil(i, j) - \rho_n \cdot \Delta soil(i, j)
\end{aligned}
}
$$

### Engineering Interpretation
The coupling between velocity and soil is the **core feedback loop** of IWD:
- Low-soil path → fast drop → more soil removed → path becomes even more attractive.
- High-soil path → slow drop → less soil removed → path becomes less attractive.

This natural positive feedback causes the algorithm to **converge to optimal paths** in routing and TSP-like problems.

---

| **Parameter** | **AFSA** | **BFOA** | **IWD** |
|---------------|----------|----------|---------|
| Population Size | `N` (fish) | `S` (bacteria) | `N_IWD` (drops) |
| Step / Velocity | `Step (S)` | `C(i)` chemotaxis | `vel(t)` dynamic |
| Stochastic Component | `Rand(·)` | `Δ(i)` unit vector | Path probability `p_i` |
| Memory / Pheromone | None | None (uses swarming) | `soil(i, j)` |
| Convergence Driver | Bulletin (global best) | Reproduction (selection) | Global soil update (best tour) |

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 AFSA Behavioural Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Initialize Fish Population]) --> Init[Set Visual V, Step S, Crowd Factor delta]
    Init --> Loop{For Each Fish}
    Loop --> Visual[Identify Neighbours within V]
    Visual --> CrowdingTest{n_f / N less than delta?}
    CrowdingTest -- Yes --> Center[Compute Centre Xc]
    Center --> FitTest{f Xc better than f Xi?}
    FitTest -- Yes --> SwarmMove[Move Toward Centre]
    FitTest -- No --> FindMax[Find Best Neighbour Xmax]
    CrowdingTest -- No --> FindMax
    FindMax --> FitTest2{f Xmax better than f Xi?}
    FitTest2 -- Yes --> FollowMove[Move Toward Xmax]
    FitTest2 -- No --> Prey[Try Prey Behaviour]
    Prey --> PreyCheck{Better Point Found?}
    PreyCheck -- Yes --> PreyMove[Move Toward Target]
    PreyCheck -- No --> RandomMove[Random Movement]
    SwarmMove --> Bulletin
    FollowMove --> Bulletin
    PreyMove --> Bulletin
    RandomMove --> Bulletin[Update Global Best Bulletin]
    Bulletin --> Converge{Convergence Met?}
    Converge -- No --> Loop
    Converge -- Yes --> Output([Return Optimal Solution])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Node identifiers use alphanumeric prefixes (Start, Init, Loop, etc.) to comply with Mermaid parsing rules. Labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting inside quotes.

## 4.2 BFOA Nested Loop Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start BFOA]) --> B[Initialize S Bacteria, theta_i]
    B --> C{Elimination-Dispersal Loop: l = 1 to N_ed}
    C --> D{Reproduction Loop: k = 1 to N_re}
    D --> E{Chemotaxis Loop: j = 1 to N_c}
    E --> F[Compute J_problem + J_cc]
    F --> G{Tumble: Generate Delta i}
    G --> H[Move: theta new = theta + C i times Delta]
    H --> I{New Fitness Better?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Swim: Continue Direction up to N_s]
    I -- No --> K[End Chemotaxis Step]
    J --> K
    K --> L{End of Chemotaxis?}
    L -- No --> E
    L -- Yes --> M[Compute J_health for all bacteria]
    M --> N[Sort Bacteria by Health]
    N --> O[Keep Top S over 2, Replace Bottom]
    O --> P{End of Reproduction?}
    P -- No --> D
    P -- Yes --> Q{For each bacterium: rand less than P_ed?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Reinitialize to Random Position]
    Q -- No --> S[Keep Current Position]
    R --> T{End of Elimination-Dispersal?}
    S --> T
    T -- No --> C
    T -- Yes --> U([Return Best Bacterium Position])
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Artificial Fish Swarm Algorithm (AFSA) and list its three main behaviours.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
AFSA is a population-based meta-heuristic optimization algorithm inspired by the collective schooling behaviour of fish, proposed by Li Xiao-Lei in 2002. Each artificial fish moves through the search space by executing three primary behaviours: **AF_Prey (Preying)** — moving toward randomly chosen positions with better food concentration; **AF_Swarm (Swarming)** — drifting toward the centre of mass of nearby fish if not crowded; and **AF_Follow (Following)** — moving toward the best-performing neighbour within the visual range. The fish also performs random movement when no better state is found. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the four main phases of the Bacteria Foraging Optimization Algorithm (BFOA).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
BFOA models the foraging lifecycle of *E. coli* bacteria through four phases: **Chemotaxis** — bacteria tumble and swim toward nutrient-rich regions via position update `θ(j+1) = θ(j) + C·Δ`; **Swarming** — bacteria release attractant and repellant signals to form coordinated groups; **Reproduction** — the healthiest half of bacteria reproduce asexually, replacing the weakest half; and **Elimination-Dispersal** — with probability `P_ed`, bacteria are randomly reinitialized to prevent premature convergence. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

### Question A (14 Marks)
**Part (a) (7 marks):** Explain in detail the Artificial Fish Swarm Algorithm (AFSA) with all its behaviours. Derive the position update equation for the prey behaviour.

**Part (b) (7 marks):** Discuss the application of AFSA in training a neural network and its advantages over gradient-based methods.

---

### Model Solution for Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — AFSA Detailed Explanation (7 marks)

**AFSA Overview (2 marks):**
AFSA simulates the social behaviour of fish in a school, where each artificial fish represents a candidate solution in the search space. The position vector `X_i = (x_1, x_2, ..., x_n)` represents the *i-th* fish's location. The fitness function `Y = f(X)` represents the food concentration. The algorithm uses three behavioural operators — preying, swarming, and following — to balance exploration and exploitation.

**Behaviour 1 — Prey (1.5 marks):**
In the prey behaviour, the fish attempts to move toward a randomly selected point `X_j` within its visual range `V` that has better food concentration. If a better point is found within `try_number` attempts, the fish moves toward it. The position update derivation proceeds as follows.

**Derivation of Prey Position Update (2.5 marks):**

The displacement vector is `d = X_j - X_i`. The Euclidean norm is `||d|| = sqrt(Σ(x_{j,k} - x_{i,k})^2)`. The unit direction is `û = d/||d||`. The step vector is `s = Rand(·) · S · û`. The new position is therefore:

$$
X_{next} = X_i + \text{Rand}(\cdot) \cdot S \cdot \frac{X_j - X_i}{\Vert X_j - X_i \Vert}
$$

**Behaviour 2 — Swarm (0.5 marks):**
Fish computes the centre of mass `X_c = ΣX_j / n_f` and moves toward it if `n_f/N < δ` and `f(X_c) < f(X_i)`.

**Behaviour 3 — Follow (0.5 marks):**
Fish identifies the best neighbour `X_max` (lowest fitness in minimization) and moves toward it if not crowded.

---

#### Part (b) — AFSA in Neural Network Training (7 marks)

**Application Context (2 marks):**
Neural network training involves finding optimal weights and biases that minimize a loss function (e.g., mean squared error). Traditional gradient-based methods like backpropagation suffer from:
1. Getting trapped in local minima.
2. Sensitivity to initial weights.
3. Poor performance on non-differentiable activation functions.

**AFSA-Based Training (3 marks):**
In AFSA-based training, each artificial fish encodes a complete set of network weights and biases. The fitness function is the inverse of the classification/regression error on the training set. The three AFSA behaviours guide the fish (weight configurations) toward configurations that yield low error.

**Advantages over Gradient Methods (2 marks):**
1. **Global Search:** AFSA does not require gradient information, avoiding local minima traps.
2. **Robustness:** Performs well on noisy, non-convex, or discontinuous loss landscapes.
3. **Parallelism:** The population-based nature allows parallel evaluation of multiple weight sets.
4. **Derivative-free:** Can train networks with non-differentiable components.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

### Question B (14 Marks)
**Part (a) (7 marks):** Describe the Bacteria Foraging Optimization Algorithm (BFOA) with its four phases. Derive the cell-to-cell signalling function.

**Part (b) (7 marks):** Explain the Intelligent Water Drop (IWD) algorithm with its key update equations. Compare IWD with ACO.

---

### Model Solution for Question B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — BFOA Detailed Explanation (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
BFOA, proposed by Kevin M. Passino in 2001, is a biologically inspired meta-heuristic that models the foraging behaviour of *E. coli* bacteria. The algorithm operates through four nested phases executed in a triple-loop structure.

**Phase 1 — Chemotaxis (1.5 marks):**
Bacteria move by alternating between **tumbling** (random direction) and **swimming** (continued in the same direction). The position update rule is:

$$
\theta_i(j+1) = \theta_i(j) + C(i) \cdot \Delta(i)
$$

If the new position improves fitness, the bacterium continues swimming up to `N_s` steps.

**Phase 2 — Swarming (1.5 marks):**
Bacteria communicate via attractant and repellant signals, creating ring-shaped swarms. The cell-to-cell signalling function is derived as follows.

**Derivation of Cell-to-Cell Signalling (2 marks):**

The squared distance is `d_i^2 = Σ(θ_m - θ_m^i)^2`. The attractant signal is `J_attract = -d_attract · exp(-w_attract · d_i^2)`. The repellant signal is `J_repellant = +h_repellant · exp(-w_repellant · d_i^2)`. Combining:

$$
J_{cc}(\theta, P) = \sum_{i=1}^{S} \left[ -d_{attract} e^{-w_{attract} d_i^2} + h_{repellant} e^{-w_{repellant} d_i^2} \right]
$$

**Phase 3 — Reproduction (0.5 marks):**
Health `J_health^i = Σ f(i, j, k, l)` is computed. The top `S/2` bacteria split, replacing the weakest `S/2`.

**Phase 4 — Elimination-Dispersal (0.5 marks):**
With probability `P_ed`, each bacterium is reinitialized to a random location, preventing local optima entrapment.

---

#### Part (b) — IWD Algorithm and Comparison with ACO (7 marks)

**IWD Algorithm Overview (2 marks):**
The IWD algorithm, proposed by Shah-Hosseini in 2007, models river formation. Each IWD has a velocity and soil amount. The algorithm uses four operators: path construction, local soil update, velocity update, and global soil update.

**Key Equations (3 marks):**
The path traversal time is `time = HU(i, j) / vel(t)`. The soil removed is `Δsoil = A_v / (B_v + C_v · time)`. The velocity updates as `vel(t+1) = vel(t) + a_v / (b_v + c_v · soil)`. The probability of selecting a path is `p_i(t) = f(soil_i) / Σ f(soil_j)`.

**Comparison with ACO (2 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **IWD** | **ACO** |
|------------|---------|---------|
| Memory element | Soil | Pheromone |
| Drop speed effect | Velocity affects soil removal | No equivalent |
| Local update rule | Continuous soil decay | Discrete pheromone deposit |
| Global update rule | Best tour reinforcement | Best tour pheromone update |
| Application bias | Path/routing problems | Discrete combinatorial |

The key difference is that IWD uses a **continuous, dynamic memory (soil)** that is modulated by velocity, while ACO uses a **discrete pheromone trail** with evaporation and deposition.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Points in KTU Examinations:**
> 1. **Confusing AFSA and PSO:** AFSA uses **three behavioural rules** (prey, swarm, follow) whereas PSO uses velocity-position updates. Examiners will deduct marks if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Missing Normalization:** The denominator `||X_j - X_i||` in AFSA prey update is **mandatory** — omitting it loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **BFOA Sign Error:** The repellant term in `J_cc` has a **positive** sign; the attractant term is **negative**. Reversing them is a frequent mistake.
> 4. **IWD Velocity Dependency:** Students often write IWD velocity as constant. It is **dynamically updated** by `a_v / (b_v + c_v · soil)`.
> 5. **Application vs Algorithm:** When asked for "applications," provide **specific engineering examples** (e.g., truss design, TSP, PID tuning) — vague answers like "optimization" lose marks.
> 6. **Forgetting Parameters:** Always mention key parameters: `V, S, δ` for AFSA; `C, N_c, N_s, N_re, N_ed, P_ed` for BFOA; `A_v, B_v, C_v, ρ_n, ρ_IWD` for IWD.

---

- Application: Neural network training, function optimization.

### BFOA Essentials
- Four phases: **Chemotaxis, Swarming, Reproduction, Elimination-Dispersal**.
- Chemotaxis update: `θ(j+1) = θ(j) + C(i) · Δ(i)`.
- Cell signalling: dual-signal (attract + repel).
- Health drives reproduction; `P_ed` controls elimination.
- Suitable for **noisy, dynamic, gradient-free** problems.
- Application: Power systems, controller tuning, harmonic estimation.

### IWD Essentials
- Key properties: **velocity** and **soil**.
- Soil removed: `Δsoil = A_v / (B_v + C_v · time)`.
- Velocity updates: `vel(t+1) = vel(t) + a_v / (b_v + c_v · soil)`.
- Probability inversely proportional to soil.
- Global reinforcement by best IWD.
- Suitable for **combinatorial/routing** problems.
- Application: TSP, vehicle routing, network routing.

### Comparative Insights
- AFSA = continuous swarm dynamics.
- BFOA = chemotactic life-cycle dynamics.
- IWD = soil-erosion path dynamics.
- All three are **population-based, derivative-free, stochastic**.

### Key Constants to Memorize
- **AFSA:** `V` (visual), `S` (step), `δ` (crowd factor), `N` (population).
- **BFOA:** `S` (population), `N_c` (chemotactic), `N_s` (swim), `N_re` (reproduction), `N_ed` (elim-disp), `P_ed` (elim-disp probability), `C(i)` (chemo step).
- **IWD:** `A_v, B_v, C_v` (soil update), `a_v, b_v, c_v` (velocity update), `ρ_n` (local), `ρ_IWD` (global), `vel_init`, `soil_init`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When answering Part B (14 marks), always structure your answer in three parts: (1) **Conceptual Introduction** with formal definition, (2) **Step-by-Step Algorithm** with equations, (3) **Engineering Application** with a specific real-world example. This pattern matches KTU 2024 valuation key expectations and guarantees full marks.
