topics.title: Case Studies - ACO and PSO for NP-Hard Problems, Routing Problems, Assignment Problems, Scheduling Problems, Subset Problems, Machine Learning Problems, Travelling Salesman Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT862 - BIO INSPIRED OPTIMIZATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies - ACO and PSO for NP-Hard Problems, Routing Problems, Assignment Problems, Scheduling Problems, Subset Problems, Machine Learning Problems, Travelling Salesman Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of NP-Hard Problems in the Bio-Inspired Context

An **NP-Hard (Non-deterministic Polynomial-time Hard) problem** is a class of computational decision/optimization problems for which no known polynomial-time algorithm exists to find the exact optimal solution. When such problems are mapped onto real-world engineering and decision-making scenarios, exact enumeration becomes computationally intractable as the problem size $n$ grows.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expected):**
> NP-Hard problems are optimization problems for which the objective is to minimize (or maximize) a cost function $f: S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ over a search space $S$ that grows exponentially (or factorially) with the input size $n$. The decision variant of such problems belongs to the NP-Complete class, implying that no polynomial-time algorithm is known (and widely believed not to exist) to solve them to guaranteed optimality.

In the context of **Bio-Inspired Optimization**, two metaheuristics stand out as the principal case-study tools for Module 4:

1. **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** — A population-based, constructive metaheuristic inspired by the pheromone-laying behavior of real ants searching for the shortest path between their nest and a food source. Introduced by **Marco Dorigo (1992)**.
2. **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)** — A population-based, trajectory-driven metaheuristic inspired by the flocking behavior of birds and schooling of fish. Introduced by **James Kennedy and Russell Eberhart (1995)**.

Both algorithms are **problem-independent**, meaning their algorithmic skeleton remains the same; only the *problem-specific representation* and *objective function evaluation* change when ported from TSP to routing, assignment, scheduling, subset, or machine learning problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy for NP-Hard Problems:**
> Imagine you are organizing a wedding with **500 guests**, and you need to seat them around **50 round tables** of **10 seats each** such that no two feuding relatives sit next to each other. There are roughly $500! / (10!^{50} \cdot 50!)$ possible seating arrangements — a number larger than the number of atoms in the observable universe. Trying every combination is impossible. A **bio-inspired algorithm** acts like a "smart guesser": it doesn't try all possibilities, but it learns from experience (pheromone trails for ants, memory of best positions for birds) to find a *very good* (not necessarily perfect) arrangement quickly.

**The Two Metaphors at a Glance:**

- **ACO = Ants leaving chemical trails.** Shorter paths accumulate more pheromone per unit time because ants complete them faster. The colony *converges* towards the shortest path.
- **PSO = Birds adjusting their flight.** Each bird remembers the best place it has personally seen (*pbest*) and the best place the flock has seen (*gbest*). It flies in a weighted direction influenced by both.

### 1.3 Taxonomy of NP-Hard Problems Studied in KTU Module 4

| Problem Class | Canonical NP-Hard Instance | Decision Variables | Search Space Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Routing** | Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) | Vehicle routes on graph | $O(n!)$ |
| **Assignment** | Quadratic Assignment Problem (QAP) | Permutation of $n$ facilities | $n!$ |
| **Scheduling** | Job Shop Scheduling Problem (JSP) | Permutation of operations | $O(n!)$ |
| **Subset** | 0/1 Knapsack | Binary selection vector | $2^n$ |
| **Machine Learning** | Neural Network Weight Tuning | Continuous weights | Continuous $\mathbb{R}^d$ |
| **Travelling Salesman** | Symmetric TSP | Hamiltonian cycle | $(n-1)!/2$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Board-Exam Insight:** When asked "Why use ACO/PSO for NP-Hard problems?", the model answer should always cite: (i) **deterministic exact methods** like Branch & Bound and Dynamic Programming scale poorly beyond $n \approx 30$–$50$ for TSP, (ii) **gradient-based methods** fail for non-differentiable, discrete, or noisy landscapes, and (iii) bio-inspired methods offer **near-optimal** solutions in **polynomial wall-clock time** with reasonable memory.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Unified Algorithmic Skeleton

Every ACO and PSO application to a specific NP-Hard problem follows a fixed four-phase pipeline:

1. **Encoding (Solution Representation):** Map the problem's decision variables into a structure the algorithm can manipulate.
2. **Initialization:** Place ants/positions/pheromones/velocities at sensible starting points.
3. **Construct / Update Loop:** Build candidate solutions and update pheromones or velocities.
4. **Pheromone Reset / Convergence Test:** Apply evaporation and re-evaluate.

### 2.2 ACO — Core Mathematical Engine

The fundamental ACO update rule for the pheromone trail $\tau_{ij}$ on edge $(i,j)$ is:

$$
\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij} + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}
$$

where the symbols are defined precisely in the formula sheet below.

**Probabilistic Transition Rule (used by an ant $k$ at node $i$ to choose next node $j$):**

$$
p_{ij}^{k}(t) = \frac{\left[\tau_{ij}(t)\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{ij}\right]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in N_{i}^{k}} \left[\tau_{il}(t)\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{il}\right]^{\beta}}
$$

### 2.3 PSO — Core Mathematical Engine

The velocity and position update equations for particle $i$ in dimension $d$:

$$
v_{id}(t+1) = w \cdot v_{id}(t) + c_1 r_1 \left(p_{id}^{best} - x_{id}(t)\right) + c_2 r_2 \left(p_{gd}^{best} - x_{id}(t)\right)
$$

$$
x_{id}(t+1) = x_{id}(t) + v_{id}(t+1)
$$

where each symbol carries the meaning in the formula sheet below.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers 80% of KTU numerical/derivation questions on ACO/PSO.**

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Domain | Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| $m$ | Number of ants (ACO) or particles (PSO) | $10 \leq m \leq 50$ | Positive integer |
| $n$ | Problem dimension (number of cities / jobs / items) | Problem-dependent | Positive integer |
| $\alpha$ | Pheromone exponent (ACO) | $0.5 \leq \alpha \leq 2.0$ | $\alpha \geq 0$ |
| $\beta$ | Heuristic exponent (ACO) | $1.0 \leq \beta \leq 5.0$ | $\beta \geq 0$ |
| $\rho$ | Pheromone evaporation rate (ACO) | $0.0 < \rho < 1.0$ | Usually $0.1$ to $0.5$ |
| $\Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}$ | Pheromone deposited by ant $k$ on edge $(i,j)$ | Depends on problem | $\Delta \tau_{ij}^{k} = Q / L_k$ (TSP) |
| $Q$ | Constant pheromone quantity (ACO) | $1 \leq Q \leq 1000$ | User-defined |
| $L_k$ | Tour length of ant $k$ (TSP) | Minimization target | $L_k > 0$ |
| $\eta_{ij}$ | Heuristic desirability ($= 1/d_{ij}$ for TSP) | $0 < \eta_{ij} \leq 1$ | Reciprocal of cost |
| $N_i^k$ | Feasible neighborhood of ant $k$ at node $i$ | Subset of nodes | Excludes visited |
| $w$ | Inertia weight (PSO) | $0.4 \leq w \leq 0.9$ | Linear decay common |
| $c_1$ | Cognitive coefficient (PSO) | $1.0 \leq c_1 \leq 2.5$ | Pulls toward pbest |
| $c_2$ | Social coefficient (PSO) | $1.0 \leq c_2 \leq 2.5$ | Pulls toward gbest |
| $r_1, r_2$ | Uniform random numbers (PSO) | $0 \leq r_1, r_2 \leq 1$ | Independent |
| $V_{max}$ | Maximum velocity (PSO) | $0.1$ to $0.5$ of search range | Clipping bound |
| $T$ | Total number of iterations | $100 \leq T \leq 5000$ | Convergence bound |
| $f(\mathbf{x})$ | Objective function to be minimized | Problem-specific | $f: \mathbb{R}^d \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ |
| $\vert S \vert$ | Cardinality of search space (upper bound) | Exponential/factorial in $n$ | $\vert S \vert = (n-1)!/2$ for TSP |

### 2.5 Problem-Specific Encoding Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for KTU)

| NP-Hard Problem | ACO Encoding | PSO Encoding | Objective $f(\mathbf{x})$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Travelling Salesman (TSP)** | Permutation of $n$ cities; edges have pheromone | Permutation with swap-operator | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} d(c_{\pi(i)}, c_{\pi(i+1)})$ |
| **Vehicle Routing (VRP)** | Routes of $K$ vehicles, depot-anchored | $K$ permutations | Total distance + penalty for overload |
| **Assignment (QAP)** | Permutation of $n$ facilities to $n$ locations | Permutation of indices | $\sum_{i,j} f_{ij} \cdot d_{\pi(i)\pi(j)}$ |
| **Scheduling (JSP)** | Operation sequence respecting precedence | Permutation of jobs | Makespan $\max\{C_j\}$ |
| **Subset (Knapsack)** | Binary vector of length $n$ | Binary-thresholded continuous vector | $-\sum v_i x_i$ subject to $\sum w_i x_i \leq W$ |
| **ML Weight Tuning** | Continuous weight vector (rarely ACO) | Continuous vector in $\mathbb{R}^d$ | MSE / cross-entropy loss |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **ACO for TSP/VRP:** Logistics companies (FedEx, Amazon) use ACO variants to plan last-mile delivery routes saving **15–25% fuel**.
> - **PSO for Neural Network Training:** Faster convergence than back-propagation on non-convex loss landscapes; used in medical image segmentation.
> - **ACO for Scheduling:** Semiconductor wafer fabrication plants use it to minimize makespan in 200+ machine environments.
> - **PSO for Knapsack:** Resource allocation in cloud-computing server provisioning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expected Pheromone Update Convergence in ACO

**Goal:** Show that, under constant ants and evaporation, the pheromone on the globally best-so-far path stabilizes and the colony *converges* (in probability) to a single tour.

We start from the discrete pheromone update recurrence:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}(t)
$$

**Step 1 — Unroll the recurrence for $T$ iterations.**

$$
\tau_{ij}(T) = (1-\rho)^{T}\cdot \tau_{ij}(0) + \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} (1-\rho)^{T-1-t} \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Take the expected value $\mathbb{E}[\cdot]$ over the random ant choices.**

$$
\mathbb{E}\left[\tau_{ij}(T)\right] = (1-\rho)^{T}\cdot \tau_{ij}(0) + \sum_{t=0}^{T-1} (1-\rho)^{T-1-t} \cdot \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}(t)\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the standard TSP deposit $\Delta \tau_{ij}^{k} = Q/L_k$ if edge $(i,j) \in T_k$, else $0$.**

$$
\mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}(t)\right] = m \cdot p_{ij}^{best} \cdot \frac{Q}{\mathbb{E}[L_{best}]}
$$

where $p_{ij}^{best}$ is the empirical frequency of edge $(i,j)$ being on the best tour.

**Step 4 — Take the limit $T \rightarrow \infty$.**

Since $\vert 1-\rho \vert < 1$ (evaporation strictly less than 1), the geometric term decays to zero:

$$
\lim_{T \to \infty} \mathbb{E}\left[\tau_{ij}(T)\right] = \frac{m \cdot p_{ij}^{best} \cdot Q}{\rho \cdot \mathbb{E}[L_{best}]}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

Every edge $(i,j)$ converges to a *constant deterministic* value proportional to the fraction of best tours that use it. This is the **Gutjahr (2000) convergence proof** that ACO converges in probability to the globally optimal tour — a key KTU 14-mark derivation.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — PSO Stability and Convergence Region

**Goal:** Find the parameter range $(w, c_1, c_2)$ under which a particle's trajectory converges (does not diverge to infinity).

**Step 1 — Combine the velocity and position equations into a single second-order recurrence.**

Substituting the velocity update into the position update:

$$
x(t+1) = x(t) + w\,v(t) + c_1 r_1 (p_{best} - x(t)) + c_2 r_2 (g_{best} - x(t))
$$

But $v(t) = x(t) - x(t-1)$, so:

$$
x(t+1) = \left(1 + w - c_1 r_1 - c_2 r_2\right) x(t) - w\,x(t-1) + c_1 r_1\, p_{best} + c_2 r_2\, g_{best}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the characteristic coefficients.**

Let $\phi_1 = c_1 r_1 + c_2 r_2$ and ignore the bounded forcing term for stability analysis (homogeneous part):

$$
x(t+1) = \left(1 + w - \phi_1\right) x(t) - w\, x(t-1)
$$

**Step 3 — Assume a trial solution $x(t) = \lambda^{t}$ and form the characteristic polynomial.**

$$
\lambda^{2} - (1 + w - \phi_1)\lambda + w = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Jury's stability criteria for a second-order linear difference equation.**

For both roots to lie inside the unit disc (convergence), the following **Trelea-type conditions** must hold:

$$
-1 < w < 1
$$

$$
0 < \phi_1 < 2(1 + w)
$$

**Step 5 — With $r_1, r_2 \sim U(0,1)$ and typical $c_1 = c_2 = 2.0$, the expected value of $\phi_1$ is $c_1 \mathbb{E}[r_1] + c_2 \mathbb{E}[r_2] = 2.0$. For convergence we need $w > 0$ and $\phi_1 < 2(1+w)$, giving the classical "sweet spot" $w \in [0.4, 0.9]$ for stable PSO.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The above is one of the most-asked 14-mark derivations. Always end with: "The convergence region is bounded by a triangle in the $(w, \phi_1)$ plane with vertices $(0,0)$, $(1,0)$, and $(1, 4)$."

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — ACO vs. PSO Across NP-Hard Problem Classes

| Problem Class | ACO Strength | PSO Strength | Preferred Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TSP** (Combinatorial / Permutation) | ★★★★★ | ★★★☆☆ | **ACO (AS/ACS variants)** | Pheromone naturally encodes graph edges |
| **VRP** (Multi-route Graph) | ★★★★★ | ★★☆☆☆ | **ACO** | Vehicle edges fit pheromone trail model |
| **QAP** (Permutation Matrix) | ★★★★☆ | ★★★☆☆ | **ACO (MMAS)** | QAP flow-distance matrices map to pheromone |
| **JSP** (Disjunctive Graph) | ★★★★☆ | ★★☆☆☆ | **ACO** | Operation sequencing leverages trail memory |
| **0/1 Knapsack** (Binary) | ★★★☆☆ | ★★★★☆ | **PSO (binary variant)** | BPSO threshold naturally produces 0/1 |
| **NN Weight Tuning** (Continuous) | ★★☆☆☆ | ★★★★★ | **PSO** | PSO excels in continuous real-valued space |
| **Feature Subset Selection** (Binary) | ★★★☆☆ | ★★★★★ | **PSO (BPSO)** | Search space is high-dimensional binary |

> [!TIP]
> **Heuristic Rule of Thumb (Frequently Asked):** Use **ACO** for *graph/path* problems; use **PSO** for *continuous / binary* problems. The crossover point is the problem's intrinsic encoding: permutations + edges → ACO, weight vectors → PSO.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — ACO on a 4-City TSP

**Problem:** Cities $A, B, C, D$ with symmetric distance matrix $d$:

$$
d = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2 & 7 & 5 \\ 2 & 0 & 4 & 8 \\ 7 & 4 & 0 & 3 \\ 5 & 8 & 3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Initial Setup:** $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 2$, $\rho = 0.1$, $Q = 1$, $m = 2$ ants, $\tau_{ij}(0) = 1$ for all edges, $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$.

**Ant 1 at A:** Probabilities to choose B, C, D:

- $p_{AB} \propto 1^{1} \cdot (1/2)^{2} = 0.250$
- $p_{AC} \propto 1^{1} \cdot (1/7)^{2} = 0.020$
- $p_{AD} \propto 1^{1} \cdot (1/5)^{2} = 0.040$
- Sum $= 0.310$

Normalized: $p_{AB} = 0.806$, $p_{AC} = 0.065$, $p_{AD} = 0.129$. Ant 1 likely chooses **B**.

Continuing this way, the colony gradually concentrates pheromone on the shortest Hamiltonian cycle: **A–B–C–D–A** with length $2 + 4 + 3 + 5 = 14$ (versus the alternative A–B–D–C–A with length $2 + 8 + 3 + 7 = 20$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Generic ACO/PSO Pipeline for NP-Hard Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start]) --> initA[Initialize Parameters: alpha beta rho Q m]
    initA --> pherInit[Set tau_ij to 1 for all edges i j]
    pherInit --> iterLoopA{Iteration t less than T}
    iterLoopA -- Yes --> placeAnts[Place all m ants at random start nodes]
    placeAnts --> solConstruct[Each ant constructs a solution using transition rule]
    solConstruct --> evalCost[Evaluate objective f_x for each ant solution]
    evalCost --> updatePher[Apply pheromone update rule with evaporation rho]
    updatePher --> updateBest[Track iteration best and global best]
    updateBest --> iterLoopA
    iterLoopA -- No --> outputA[Output global best solution]
    outputA --> stopA([Stop])
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Generic PSO Pipeline for NP-Hard Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start]) --> initB[Initialize swarm: positions x_i and velocities v_i]
    initB --> setCoeff[Set w c1 c2 Vmax for each particle]
    setCoeff --> evalObj[Evaluate objective f_x_i for all particles]
    evalObj --> setPbest[Set personal best pbest_i for each particle]
    setPbest --> setGbest[Set global best gbest across swarm]
    setGbest --> iterLoopB{Iteration t less than T}
    iterLoopB -- Yes --> updateV[Update velocity v_i using w c1 c2]
    updateV --> clipV[Clip velocity to Vmax range]
    clipV --> updateX[Update position x_i = x_i plus v_i]
    updateX --> evalNew[Re-evaluate objective f_x_i]
    evalNew --> updPbest[Update pbest_i if improved]
    updPbest --> updGbest[Update gbest if improved]
    updGbest --> iterLoopB
    iterLoopB -- No --> outputB[Output gbest as best solution]
    outputB --> stopB([Stop])
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Problem-to-Method Selection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    problemIn[Given NP-Hard Problem] --> classify{Encoding Type?}
    classify -- Permutation or Graph --> acoPath[Use ACO Variants]
    classify -- Continuous Real Vector --> psoPath[Use PSO Variants]
    classify -- Binary Vector --> bpsoPath[Use Binary PSO]
    acoPath --> acoVar{Subtype?}
    acoVar -- TSP --> acoACS[Ant Colony System]
    acoVar -- VRP --> acoMMAS[Max-Min Ant System]
    acoVar -- QAP or JSP --> acoElit[Elitist ACO]
    psoPath --> psoVar{Subtype?}
    psoVar -- Continuous Optimization --> psoStd[Standard PSO]
    psoVar -- Constrained Problem --> psoCPSO[Constriction Coefficient PSO]
    psoVar -- Multi-Objective --> psoMOPSO[MOPSO with Archive]
    bpsoPath --> bpsoOut[Binary PSO with Sigmoid Transfer]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Pheromone Update Loop (Ant-Cycle System)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    antTour[Ant k completes a full tour T_k of length L_k] --> computeDelta[Compute delta tau_ij k = Q divided by L_k for all edges in T_k]
    computeDelta --> evapLoop[For all edges i j: apply evaporation tau_ij times one minus rho]
    evapLoop --> depositLoop[For all edges in T_k: add delta tau_ij k to tau_ij]
    depositLoop --> globalBest[If T_k is best so far: multiply delta by elitist weight e]
    globalBest --> nextIter[Proceed to next iteration]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — PSO Vector Geometry (Velocity Decomposition)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    currPos[Current Position x_i] --> vPrev[Previous Velocity w times v]
    currPos --> vCog[Cognitive Term c1 r1 times pbest minus x_i]
    currPos --> vSoc[Social Term c2 r2 times gbest minus x_i]
    vPrev --> vNew[New Velocity v_i new]
    vCog --> vNew
    vSoc --> vNew
    vNew --> clip[Clip to Vmax Bounds]
    clip --> newPos[New Position x_i new equals x_i plus v_i new]
    newPos --> currPos
```

### 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture — ACO/PSO Integrated Solver for TSP/VRP

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph inputLayer[INPUT LAYER]
        problemDef[Problem Definition: Graph G V E and cost matrix d_ij]
        paramCfg[Parameter Config: alpha beta rho m T w c1 c2]
    end
    subgraph algoCore[ALGORITHM CORE]
        encoder[Solution Encoder: Permutation to Vector mapping]
        constructor[Solution Constructor: Ants or Particles]
        evaluator[Objective Evaluator: f_x]
        memory[Memory: Pheromone Matrix tau_ij or pbest gbest]
        updater[Update Engine: Pheromone or Velocity]
    end
    subgraph outputLayer[OUTPUT LAYER]
        bestSol[Best Solution Found]
        convCurve[Convergence Curve]
        perfMetrics[Performance Metrics: Best Mean Std Dev]
    end
    problemDef --> encoder
    paramCfg --> encoder
    encoder --> constructor
    memory --> constructor
    constructor --> evaluator
    evaluator --> updater
    updater --> memory
    evaluator --> bestSol
    evaluator --> convCurve
    evaluator --> perfMetrics
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*"Define an NP-Hard problem. Why are bio-inspired algorithms like ACO and PSO preferred over exact methods for solving such problems?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):**

An **NP-Hard problem** is an optimization problem for which the search space grows exponentially or factorially with input size $n$, and no polynomial-time algorithm is known to find the guaranteed optimal solution. Examples include TSP, VRP, and QAP. **Bio-inspired algorithms (ACO, PSO)** are preferred because: (i) they are *polynomial-time* in wall-clock, (ii) they handle *non-differentiable, discrete, and noisy* landscapes where gradient methods fail, and (iii) they deliver *near-optimal* solutions of practical engineering value without exhaustive enumeration.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*"Differentiate between Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) and Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) in terms of solution construction mechanism and information sharing."*

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):**

| Aspect | ACO | PSO |
|---|---|---|
| **Biological basis** | Ants' pheromone trails | Birds' flocking behavior |
| **Solution construction** | Probabilistic, edge-by-edge using $p_{ij}^{k} = [\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha}[\eta_{ij}]^{\beta} / \Sigma$ | Position update using velocity vector |
| **Information sharing** | Implicit via stigmergy (pheromone matrix $\tau$) | Explicit via $g_{best}$ (global best particle) |
| **Memory** | Distributed across edges | Per-particle $p_{best}$ and swarm $g_{best}$ |
| **Best suited for** | Combinatorial/graph problems | Continuous/binary problems |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): ACO for NP-Hard Problems**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*"Apply Ant Colony Optimization to the Travelling Salesman Problem. Explain the algorithm with the transition rule, pheromone update, and evaporation mechanism. Also discuss how the same ACO framework is adapted for the Quadratic Assignment Problem (QAP)."*

##### **Part (a) — ACO Algorithm for TSP [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Problem Encoding:**
Cities are nodes of a complete graph $G = (V, E)$ with $|V| = n$. A solution is a Hamiltonian cycle (permutation) $\pi = (\pi(1), \pi(2), \ldots, \pi(n))$. Objective: minimize total tour length $L(\pi) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} d(\pi(i), \pi(i+1))$ with $\pi(n+1) = \pi(1)$.

**Step 2 — Initialization:**
Set $\tau_{ij}(0) = \tau_0$ (a small constant, e.g., $\tau_0 = 1$) for every edge. Set $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ as the heuristic visibility.

**Step 3 — Ant Construction (Transition Rule):**
At node $i$, ant $k$ chooses next node $j \in N_i^{k}$ (unvisited neighbors) with probability:

$$
p_{ij}^{k}(t) = \frac{\left[\tau_{ij}(t)\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{ij}\right]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in N_i^{k}} \left[\tau_{il}(t)\right]^{\alpha} \cdot \left[\eta_{il}\right]^{\beta}}
$$

**Step 4 — Local Pheromone Update (optional in ACS):**
After each move, $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1 - \xi) \tau_{ij} + \xi \tau_0$ to encourage exploration.

**Step 5 — Global Pheromone Update (Ant-Cycle System):**
After all $m$ ants complete tours:

$$
\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1 - \rho)\,\tau_{ij} + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}, \quad \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k} = \begin{cases} Q/L_k & \text{if edge } (i,j) \in \pi_k \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 6 — Repeat** Steps 3–5 for $T$ iterations; output the best tour found.

##### **Part (b) — ACO Adaptation for QAP [7 Marks]**

The **Quadratic Assignment Problem** assigns $n$ facilities to $n$ locations to minimize:

$$
f(\pi) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} f_{ij}\cdot d_{\pi(i)\pi(j)}
$$

where $f_{ij}$ = flow between facilities $i,j$ and $d_{kl}$ = distance between locations $k,l$.

**ACO Adaptation Steps:**

1. **Encoding:** A solution is a permutation $\pi$ of facility indices; pheromone $\tau_{ij}$ now represents the *desirability of placing facility $i$ at location $j$*.
2. **Heuristic:** $\eta_{ij} = 1 / \sum_{k} f_{ik}\cdot d_{j\pi(k)}$, a greedy local estimate.
3. **Construction:** Ant builds the permutation greedily using $\tau_{ij}^{\alpha}\eta_{ij}^{\beta}$.
4. **Pheromone Update:** Edges of the best permutation get deposits $Q/f(\pi^*)$.
5. **Variants Used:** **MMAS (Max-Min Ant System)** with pheromone bounds $[\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$ to prevent premature stagnation is the standard choice for QAP.

**Discussion:** Unlike TSP where pheromone lives on edges between cities, in QAP pheromone lives on *facility-to-location* assignments, reflecting the binary decision nature of the problem.

#### **Question B (14 Marks): PSO for NP-Hard Problems**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*"Explain the Particle Swarm Optimization algorithm and derive its stability/convergence conditions. Discuss with examples how PSO (and its binary variant BPSO) is applied to (i) continuous optimization in neural network training, and (ii) the 0/1 Knapsack subset problem."*

##### **Part (a) — PSO Algorithm and Stability Derivation [7 Marks]**

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Initialize** a swarm of $m$ particles with random positions $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and velocities $v_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$.
2. **Evaluate** objective $f(x_i)$ for each particle.
3. **Set** $p_{best,i} = x_i$ and $g_{best} = \arg\min_i f(x_i)$.
4. **Update** for each particle and dimension:

$$
v_{id}(t+1) = w\,v_{id}(t) + c_1 r_1(p_{id}^{best} - x_{id}(t)) + c_2 r_2(p_{gd}^{best} - x_{id}(t))
$$

$$
x_{id}(t+1) = x_{id}(t) + v_{id}(t+1)
$$

5. **Clip** $v_{id}$ to $[-V_{max}, V_{max}]$ to prevent explosion.
6. **Update** $p_{best}$ and $g_{best}$ based on new positions.
7. **Repeat** for $T$ iterations; output $g_{best}$.

**Stability Derivation (Jury's Criterion):**

Combining velocity and position updates:

$$
x(t+1) = (1 + w - \phi_1)x(t) - w\,x(t-1) + \phi_1 \bar{x}
$$

where $\phi_1 = c_1 r_1 + c_2 r_2$ and $\bar{x}$ is a weighted attractor. The characteristic polynomial:

$$
\lambda^2 - (1 + w - \phi_1)\lambda + w = 0
$$

Applying Jury's criteria yields the **convergence region**:
- $-1 < w < 1$
- $0 < \phi_1 < 2(1+w)$
- $\phi_1 > -2(1+w)$

This triangle in $(w, \phi_1)$ space guarantees that particle trajectories converge without divergence. The recommended **Clerc–Kennedy constriction factor** $\chi = 0.7298$ with $c_1 = c_2 = 2.05$ guarantees convergence.

##### **Part (b) — PSO for Neural Network Training and 0/1 Knapsack [7 Marks]**

**(i) PSO for Neural Network Weight Tuning:**
A multi-layer perceptron (MLP) with $d$ weights is trained by PSO directly. Each particle $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ encodes all weights and biases. The objective is the mean squared error (or cross-entropy):

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\left(y_k - \hat{y}_k(\mathbf{x})\right)^2
$$

PSO searches this continuous, non-convex, multi-modal landscape. **Advantages over back-propagation (BP):** (a) no need to compute gradients, (b) escapes local minima due to swarm diversity, (c) parallel evaluation. Reported convergence is faster by 30–50% on small-to-medium networks.

**(ii) Binary PSO (BPSO) for 0/1 Knapsack:**
Position values are constrained to $\{0,1\}$ via a sigmoid transfer:

$$
x_{id}(t+1) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \mathrm{rand}() < S(v_{id}(t+1)) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

where $S(v) = 1/(1 + e^{-v})$ is the logistic function and velocity controls the probability of selecting an item. The objective is to maximize profit:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i x_i \quad \text{subject to} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i \leq W
$$

Infeasible solutions are penalized by adding $\lambda \cdot \max(0, \sum w_i x_i - W)$ to the objective. BPSO efficiently navigates the $2^n$ binary space and finds near-optimal knapsacks in $O(m \cdot n \cdot T)$ time.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the roles of $\alpha$ and $\beta$** — $\alpha$ weights *learned* information (pheromone), $\beta$ weights *greedy* information (heuristic). Reversing these in a numerical problem will cost 2 marks.
> 2. **Always state the typical range of parameters** ($\alpha = 1, \beta = 2, \rho = 0.1, w = 0.7, c_1 = c_2 = 2.0$) — vague answers lose marks.
> 3. **In PSO derivations, mention the constriction factor $\chi = 0.7298$** at least once; KTU examiners award bonus marks for naming it.
> 4. **For BPSO**, the sigmoid transfer function is *mandatory*; without it, the answer is incomplete.
> 5. **TSP tour length $L_k$ appears in the denominator** of $\Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}$ — students often put it in the numerator, which is the most common error.
> 6. **In the convergence derivation**, end with the *triangular region in $(w, \phi_1)$ plane*; numerical range alone is not enough.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Cover These and You Cover the Module:**

- ✅ **NP-Hard** = no polynomial exact algorithm; search space is exponential/factorial in input size $n$.
- ✅ **ACO** is best for **combinatorial/graph** problems; **PSO** is best for **continuous/binary** problems.
- ✅ **ACO transition rule:** $p_{ij}^{k} \propto \tau_{ij}^{\alpha}\eta_{ij}^{\beta}$ — pheromone × heuristic, normalized.
- ✅ **ACO pheromone update:** $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\rho)\tau_{ij} + \sum_k \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}$ with $\Delta \tau_{ij}^{k} = Q/L_k$ (Ant-Cycle).
- ✅ **PSO velocity update:** $v_{id} = w v_{id} + c_1 r_1(p_{best,id} - x_{id}) + c_2 r_2(g_{best,d} - x_{id})$.
- ✅ **PSO position update:** $x_{id} \leftarrow x_{id} + v_{id}$; clip to $[-V_{max}, V_{max}]$.
- ✅ **PSO stability:** Jury's criteria give $-1 < w < 1$ and $0 < \phi_1 < 2(1+w)$; constriction $\chi = 0.7298$.
- ✅ **BPSO** uses sigmoid $S(v) = 1/(1+e^{-v})$ to map continuous velocity to binary position; used for Knapsack/feature selection.
- ✅ **TSP objective:** $L(\pi) = \sum_{i} d(c_{\pi(i)}, c_{\pi(i+1)})$; search space $= (n-1)!/2$.
- ✅ **QAP objective:** $\sum_{i,j} f_{ij} d_{\pi(i)\pi(j)}$; uses MMAS variant of ACO.
- ✅ **VRP** = multi-vehicle TSP; depot is the start/end node of each subroute; capacity constraints.
- ✅ **JSP (Job Shop Scheduling)** is solved with disjunctive-graph ACO; objective = minimize makespan $\max C_j$.
- ✅ **0/1 Knapsack** is solved with BPSO; infeasible solutions get a penalty term.
- ✅ **ML weight tuning** uses PSO with MSE/cross-entropy as fitness; no gradient required.
- ✅ **Gutjahr's theorem** proves ACO converges in probability to optimal tour (state this in 14-mark answers).
- ✅ **Typical parameter values to memorize:** $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 2$, $\rho \in [0.1, 0.5]$, $w = 0.7$, $c_1 = c_2 = 2.0$, $Q = 1$ (or $Q = L_{best}$ for Ant-Cycle).
- ✅ **Differences tabulated:** ACO uses stigmergy (indirect communication via pheromone); PSO uses direct memory sharing ($p_{best}, g_{best}$).
- ✅ **Real-world deployment:** Amazon/FedEx routing (ACO), cloud load balancing (PSO), chip fabrication scheduling (ACO), medical image segmentation (PSO).
- ✅ **Draw both flowcharts** — the ACO pipeline and the PSO pipeline — without looking, for full marks in diagram-based questions.
